VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Pythagorean Theorem
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why a² + b² = c² works, explained visually in under 2 minutes.

scenes.narration: Welcome. In this lesson we’ll understand why the Pythagorean theorem is true — not just memorize it.
In a right triangle, the two shorter sides are called the legs, and the longest side is called the hypotenuse.
The theorem says: if you square the lengths of the legs and add them, you get the square of the hypotenuse.
Here’s the key idea. Build a square whose sides each equal a plus b. Its total area is (a plus b) squared.
Now fit four copies of the right triangle inside that square. Their hypotenuses form an inner tilted square, with area c squared.
Each triangle has area one-half a b, so four of them together have area 2 a b.
The outer square equals the four triangles plus the inner square. So a squared plus 2 a b plus b squared equals 2 a b plus c squared.
Subtract 2 a b from both sides and we’re left with a squared plus b squared equals c squared.
That’s the whole proof. Memorize the picture, not the formula — it always comes back.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Quadratic Formula, Intuitively
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Where the quadratic formula actually comes from — completing the square, step by step.

scenes.narration: Every quadratic equation — a x squared plus b x plus c equals zero — has the same solution pattern. Let’s see why.

visual.subtitle: Not magic. Just completing the square.

scenes.narration: Start with a x squared plus b x plus c equals zero. Our goal is to isolate x.
Divide everything by a so the x squared term has no coefficient.
Move the constant to the other side.
To complete the square, we add b over 2 a, all squared, to both sides.
The left side is now a perfect square: x plus b over 2 a, squared.
Take the square root of both sides. Remember the plus-or-minus.
Solve for x, and out pops the famous formula.
The quantity under the square root, b squared minus 4 a c, tells you how many solutions there are. Positive means two, zero means one, negative means none in the real numbers.
Now you know not just the formula, but where it comes from. That’s how mathematicians remember it.

visual.subtitle: Remember the derivation, not the formula.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: What Slope Really Means
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Rise over run, positive vs negative, and why slope matters in real life.

scenes.narration: Slope is just a number that tells you how steep a line is. It’s rise over run.
If you go one step to the right and the line goes up by 2, the slope is 2.
Positive slopes go up. Negative slopes go down. Zero means horizontal. Undefined means vertical.
You can always compute slope from two points — y two minus y one, over x two minus x one.
Slope in real life: miles per hour is a slope. Dollars per hour. Heartbeats per minute. Anything "per" something is a slope.
That’s it. Rise over run. You’ll see it again in calculus — the derivative is just slope at a single point.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: What a Derivative Actually Is
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Zooming in on a curve until it looks like a line — that line's slope is the derivative.

scenes.narration: A derivative sounds scary, but it’s just slope at a single point.
Pick any curve. Between two points, the slope is their rise over run — that’s called the average rate of change.
Now slide the two points closer together. The slope of the line between them starts to hug the curve.
In the limit, as the two points become one, you get the slope of the tangent line. That’s the derivative.
For a parabola y equals x squared, this limit equals 2 x. At x equals 3, the slope is 6. That’s it.
Derivatives tell you how things change at a specific moment — velocity from position, acceleration from velocity, growth rate at a point in time.
Remember: derivative equals slope equals rate of change. Same idea, three names.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: What an Integral Actually Is
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: The area under a curve — why that idea shows up everywhere in physics and statistics.

scenes.narration: An integral is the area under a curve. That’s the one-sentence version.
Start by slicing the area under a curve into thin rectangles, each of width delta x.
Add up their heights times their widths. That approximates the area.
Now make the rectangles thinner and thinner. In the limit, the approximation becomes exact.
Why does this matter? If f of x is speed, the area under the curve is distance traveled. If f of x is probability density, the area is probability.

visual.text: Area under the curve = total accumulated.

scenes.narration: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus says integration and differentiation are opposites. Take a derivative, then integrate — you get back where you started.
Remember: integrals accumulate, derivatives chop up. Together they describe all of change.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Fractions Without Confusion
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why common denominators matter and how to add, subtract, multiply, and divide fractions.

scenes.narration: Fractions trip people up, but they’re just divisions written differently.
To add fractions, both pieces must be the same size. That’s called a common denominator.
To multiply, just multiply straight across. Top times top, bottom times bottom.
To divide, flip the second fraction and multiply. This is called multiplying by the reciprocal.
Always simplify at the end. Divide top and bottom by their common factor.
That’s all there is. Common denominator for adding, straight across for multiplying, flip-and-multiply for dividing.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Exponent Rules in 2 Minutes
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Product, quotient, power, and zero-exponent rules — and why each one makes sense.

scenes.narration: Exponents look scary, but every rule comes from counting factors.
x to the m means x multiplied by itself m times. So x squared times x cubed means two copies times three copies — five copies total.
Dividing subtracts exponents. Canceling copies.
A power of a power multiplies exponents. Three groups of two copies equals six copies.
Anything to the zero is 1, because x over x is 1 and x to the 0 must fit that rule.
A negative exponent is one over a positive. That makes the quotient rule work even when n is bigger than m.
Every exponent rule comes from the same idea — counting copies. Remember that and you’ll never forget them.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: How StudyBoost Knows What You Know
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: A plain-English explanation of Bayesian Knowledge Tracing — the engine powering your Mastery Map.

scenes.narration: Ever wonder how an app can tell whether you actually know something? StudyBoost uses something called Bayesian Knowledge Tracing.
Every topic gets a number between 0 and 1 — the probability that you’ve mastered it.
Each time you answer a question, the number updates using four values: how likely you knew it before, how fast you learn, how often you guess, and how often you slip.
Get it right? The number goes up. Get it wrong? It goes down. But the update also accounts for lucky guesses and careless mistakes.
StudyBoost also knows that a calculus skill fades faster than an arithmetic fact, so it forgets at different speeds per topic.
This is the same family of techniques published adaptive-learning research uses. Running right in your browser.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Active Recall: The Most-Supported Study Method
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why cognitive-science research keeps finding active recall beats re-reading — and how to do it.

scenes.narration: Decades of cognitive-science research keep pointing at one technique as unusually effective: active recall.
Re-reading notes feels productive. But studies show you forget most of it within a day.
Instead, close the notes and try to say the answer out loud, or write it down without looking.

visual.text: Ask a question. Answer it cold.

scenes.narration: Struggling to remember is the part that actually builds the memory. That struggle is called a "desirable difficulty" in learning science.
StudyBoost’s flashcards, quizzes, and daily warmup are all active recall tools. Use them over notes, every time.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Spaced Repetition, Explained Simply
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why reviewing at the right time is far more powerful than cramming.

scenes.narration: Your brain forgets things on a predictable curve. The trick is to review just before you’d forget.

visual.subtitle: The right review at the right time.

scenes.narration: Learn something today. Review it tomorrow. Then in 3 days. Then 7. Then 14. Each successful review stretches the next interval.
Miss a review? The interval resets and you start again. The schedule adapts to you.

visual.text: Miss one → reset. Nail it → extend.

scenes.narration: StudyBoost schedules reviews using the SM-2 algorithm, fine-tuned by your current mastery probability from BKT.
The result: ten minutes a day beats two hours once a week for long-term retention. Decades of study-science research converge on this.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: How to Read an Essay Prompt
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Breaking down any essay prompt into exactly what's being asked so your answer can't miss the point.

scenes.narration: Most lost essay points aren’t about grammar. They’re about not answering the question. Here’s how to read any prompt.
Find the verb. "Describe", "analyze", "compare", "evaluate" — each one asks for something different.
Describe means summarize. Analyze means break down. Compare needs similarities AND differences. Evaluate needs a judgment with reasons.
Underline the topic. Circle any constraints: word count, time period, point of view.
Rewrite the prompt as a question you’re answering. If you can’t, you don’t understand it yet.

visual.text: If you can't rephrase it, you don't get it.

scenes.narration: A 30-second prompt analysis saves 30 minutes of wrong-direction writing. Every time.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: How Binary Works
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Base-2, bits, and why computers count in 0s and 1s.

scenes.narration: Computers represent everything — numbers, letters, images, videos — with just two symbols: 0 and 1.
In base 10, the digits go ones, tens, hundreds — powers of 10. In base 2, they go ones, twos, fours, eights — powers of 2.
Each 0 or 1 is called a bit. Eight bits together is a byte, which can hold 256 different values.
To convert a decimal number to binary, divide by 2 repeatedly and write down the remainders from bottom to top.
Computers use binary because transistors have two reliable states: on or off. Every program you’ve ever run is ultimately flipping tiny switches.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Fractions, Without the Fear
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why fractions work the way they do, using just circles and intuition.

scenes.narration: A fraction is two numbers: one tells you how many pieces, the other tells you how big the pieces are.
The bottom number is called the denominator. It names the size of each piece. The bigger the denominator, the smaller the piece.
The top number is called the numerator. It counts how many of those pieces you have.
To add fractions, the pieces have to be the same size. One-half plus one-third requires finding a common denominator.
To multiply fractions, multiply across: top times top, bottom times bottom. That’s because you’re taking a fraction of a fraction.
Remember: fractions are just counts of equal pieces. Everything else follows.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: What a Logarithm Is
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Logs demystified: they're the opposite of exponents.

scenes.narration: A logarithm answers the question: to what power do I raise a base to get a number?
For example, log base 10 of 1000 equals 3, because 10 cubed is 1000.
Logs turn multiplication into addition. The log of a product equals the sum of the logs.
This property is why log scales compress enormous ranges. Earthquake magnitudes, sound decibels, and pH are all logarithmic.

visual.text: Magnitude 7 quake is 10× stronger than magnitude 6

scenes.narration: Natural logs use base e. They show up wherever something grows continuously, like compound interest or population growth.
Memorize: a logarithm is just an exponent in hiding.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Probability, in Under Two Minutes
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Favorable outcomes over total outcomes — when and why.

scenes.narration: Probability measures how likely an event is. It’s always between zero and one.
For equally-likely outcomes, probability is favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes.
A fair coin has two outcomes, one of which is heads. So the probability of heads is one-half.
For independent events, multiply. The chance of two heads in a row is one-half times one-half, which is one-quarter.
For mutually exclusive events, add. The chance of rolling a 1 or a 2 is one-sixth plus one-sixth, which is one-third.
Two rules: multiply for AND with independence, add for OR when disjoint. The rest is careful counting.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Systems of Equations
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Two equations, two unknowns — substitution or elimination.

scenes.narration: When you have two unknowns, you need two equations to pin them down.
Consider x plus y equals 10, and x minus y equals 2. Graphed, each is a line. The solution is where the lines cross.
Method one: substitution. Solve one equation for a variable and plug into the other.
Method two: elimination. Add the equations to cancel a variable.
Either way, the answer is x equals 6, y equals 4. The two lines intersect at the point (6, 4).
Pick substitution when one variable is already isolated. Pick elimination when a variable lines up to cancel.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: What a Limit Is
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: The foundation of calculus: approaching without arriving.

scenes.narration: A limit describes where a function is heading as its input approaches a certain value, even if it never quite gets there.
Consider f of x equals x squared. As x approaches 3, x squared approaches 9. That’s the limit.
Limits matter when a function has a hole. Consider (x squared minus 1) over (x minus 1). At x equals 1, both sides are zero — undefined.
But the function equals x plus 1 everywhere else. So its limit as x approaches 1 is 2, even though the function is undefined there.
This idea — approaching a value without reaching it — is what lets calculus measure instantaneous rates of change.

visual.text: Derivatives are limits. Integrals are limits.

scenes.narration: A limit is the value a function is heading toward. Master this, and calculus makes sense.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Photosynthesis in 90 Seconds
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: How plants turn sunlight, water, and CO₂ into sugar.

scenes.narration: Plants eat sunlight. That’s not a metaphor. Let’s see how.
Plants take in carbon dioxide from the air through tiny pores on their leaves, and water from the soil through their roots.
Inside the leaf, chlorophyll captures sunlight and uses that energy to split water and combine the parts with carbon dioxide.
The output is glucose — the plant’s food — and oxygen, which plants release into the air.
Every atom of oxygen in the air you breathe came from a plant doing this reaction. Every calorie in your body traces back to it.
Photosynthesis: turning sunlight into food. The foundation of nearly all life.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The pH Scale
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why lemon juice is acidic and bleach is basic — logarithmically.

scenes.narration: The pH scale tells you how acidic or basic a solution is. It runs from 0 to 14.
pH is defined as negative log base 10 of the hydrogen ion concentration.
A pH of 7 is neutral — pure water. Below 7 is acidic. Above 7 is basic.
Because it’s logarithmic, each unit is ten times more acidic or basic. Lemon juice at pH 2 is 100,000 times more acidic than water.
Your blood sits tightly around pH 7.4. A shift of just 0.2 can be fatal. That’s why your body has buffering systems.
pH is a log-scale of acidity. Small changes mean big differences.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Newton’s Three Laws
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Inertia, F=ma, action-reaction — what they mean, not just what they say.

scenes.narration: Isaac Newton summarized motion with three laws. They describe how objects behave when forces act on them.
First law: an object at rest stays at rest. An object in motion stays in motion, in a straight line, unless acted upon by a force.

visual.text: 1st: objects resist change. This resistance is called inertia.

scenes.narration: Second law: the force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration. Push harder, accelerate more. Heavier object, accelerate less.
Third law: for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. When you push off the ground to jump, the ground pushes you up.

visual.text: 3rd: forces come in pairs. Always opposite. Always equal.

scenes.narration: These three laws explain nearly all everyday motion: walking, driving, rockets, even orbits, at non-relativistic speeds.

visual.text: Cars, planes, baseballs, satellites — all Newton.

scenes.narration: Newton’s laws are a compact engine for predicting how things move. Master them and the physical world becomes legible.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: How DNA Works
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: The double helix, bases, and how instructions get read.

scenes.narration: DNA is a long twisted ladder — a double helix — that stores the instructions for every living thing.
The rungs of the ladder are made of four chemical bases: A, T, C, and G. A always pairs with T, and C always pairs with G.
The order of these letters spells out genes — instructions for building proteins, which run everything in your body.
A human genome is about 3 billion letters long, packed into 46 chromosomes inside almost every cell.
When a cell divides, it copies its DNA letter by letter. Mistakes happen — that’s where evolution and some diseases come from.

visual.text: Copy errors = mutations = evolution and cancer

scenes.narration: DNA: a four-letter code, copied trillions of times, that built you.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: How Electricity Flows
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Voltage, current, resistance — and Ohm's law as the glue.

scenes.narration: Electricity is the flow of electrons through a material. Three quantities describe it: voltage, current, and resistance.
Voltage is the push. Think of it as the pressure behind a water hose.
Current is the flow rate — how many electrons pass a point per second. Measured in amperes.
Resistance is how much the material fights the flow. A thin wire resists more than a thick one.
Ohm’s law ties them together: voltage equals current times resistance.
Power — how fast energy is used — is voltage times current. That’s how we rate light bulbs in watts.
Electricity: pressure pushes electrons through resistance. That’s the whole game.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Reading the Periodic Table
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why the table is arranged the way it is.

scenes.narration: The periodic table is organized by atomic number — the count of protons in each element’s nucleus.
Each row is called a period. As you move right, atoms gain one more proton and one more electron.
Elements in the same column have similar chemical behavior because their outermost electron shells look the same.
The leftmost column — alkali metals like lithium and sodium — react violently with water. The rightmost — noble gases like helium — barely react at all.
The table’s shape reflects how electrons fill atomic orbitals. It’s a direct fingerprint of quantum mechanics.

visual.text: s, p, d, f orbitals → table’s blocks

scenes.narration: The periodic table isn’t just a list. It’s a map of electron behavior — and it predicts chemistry.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Supply and Demand
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why prices move — the first tool of microeconomics.

scenes.narration: Prices aren’t set by sellers alone or buyers alone. They emerge from the interaction of supply and demand.
The demand curve slopes down: when price drops, people want more. When price rises, people want less.
The supply curve slopes up: when price rises, producers make more. When price drops, they make less.
The price that clears the market — where the two curves cross — is called the equilibrium price.
If demand rises but supply doesn’t keep up, prices go up. Concert tickets, sneakers, gas during a shortage — all examples.

visual.text: Scarce supply + high demand = high price

scenes.narration: Supply and demand: the most portable tool in economics. It shows up in labor, housing, crypto, dating apps.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Writing a Thesis Statement
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: The engine of every essay — specific, arguable, provable.

scenes.narration: A thesis is the single sentence that tells the reader what your essay argues. It’s the engine of the whole paper.

visual.subtitle: One sentence. Does a lot of work.

scenes.narration: A weak thesis is a topic: "This essay is about climate change." That’s not an argument.

visual.text: Weak: "This essay is about climate change."

scenes.narration: A strong thesis makes a claim that someone could disagree with: "Climate policy should prioritize nuclear energy over solar."
Good theses have three qualities: specific, arguable, and provable within your essay’s scope.
Every paragraph should support the thesis. If a paragraph doesn’t advance your argument, cut it or rewrite it.
The thesis is a contract with the reader. Make it specific, bold, and worth reading.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Recursion, Explained
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: A function that calls itself — and how it actually terminates.

scenes.narration: Recursion is when a function calls itself. It sounds circular, but it works — if you have a base case.
Classic example: factorial. The factorial of n equals n times the factorial of n minus 1.
That definition references itself. What stops the recursion? A base case. We define 0 factorial to be 1.
So to compute 4 factorial: we call 4 times 3 factorial, which calls 3 times 2 factorial, down to the base case. Then it unwinds.
Recursion is natural for tree structures, search, and divide-and-conquer. Quicksort, tree traversal, fractals all use it.
Two rules: always have a base case, and make sure each call moves closer to it.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Big O Notation
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why some algorithms scale and others crash.

scenes.narration: Big O describes how an algorithm’s runtime grows as the input gets larger. It ignores constants.
O of 1 means constant time. Looking up a key in a hash table is O of 1 — doesn’t matter how big the table is.
O of n means linear. Scanning through a list of n items takes time proportional to n.
O of n log n is the speed of good sorts like merge sort and quicksort. Much better than n squared.
O of n squared is nested loops. Fine for small n, painful for large n. 10,000 items means 100 million operations.
O of 2 to the n is exponential. Adding one more item doubles the work. Unusable beyond tiny inputs.
Big O is how engineers talk about scale. Pick the right growth class and your code survives real data.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Spaced Repetition Works
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why reviewing just before you forget is the most efficient way to learn.

scenes.narration: Your brain forgets in a predictable curve. Something learned today is half-gone within a day if not reviewed.
Each time you successfully recall something, the forgetting curve flattens. Memory traces strengthen with retrieval.

visual.text: Recall strengthens memory. Rereading does not.

scenes.narration: Spaced repetition times each review just before you would forget. The intervals grow: 1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 2 weeks, 1 month.
Compared to cramming, spaced repetition produces three to five times better long-term retention for the same study time.
Apps like Anki schedule this automatically. StudyBoost’s flashcards use SM-2 with mastery-aware adjustments.
The most efficient study isn’t the longest — it’s the best-timed. Use spaced repetition.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Scientific Method
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Hypothesis, test, revise — how science corrects its own errors.

scenes.narration: The scientific method is how we figure out what’s true. It’s a loop, not a checklist.
Step one: observe. Notice something strange, unexpected, or incomplete in current understanding.
Step two: form a hypothesis. A precise claim that could be wrong.
Step three: test. Design an experiment that would disprove your hypothesis if it’s false.

visual.text: 3. Test — try to break your own idea

scenes.narration: Step four: revise. Update your belief based on what you found. Then repeat.
The key insight: science seeks disproof, not proof. Karl Popper called it falsifiability.

visual.text: A real hypothesis can be wrong.

scenes.narration: The scientific method isn’t about lab coats. It’s about honest error correction.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Why Active Recall Beats Rereading
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: The retrieval effect: practice testing yourself, not reviewing notes.

scenes.narration: Most students study by rereading notes or highlighting. This feels productive. It isn’t.

visual.subtitle: The study technique with the most evidence

scenes.narration: Research is unambiguous: retrieving information — testing yourself — produces far more durable memory than reviewing.
The mechanism: each successful recall is a rehearsal. Your brain rebuilds the memory trace, strengthening it.
Practical: cover your notes, try to recall the main points, then check. Or use flashcards, practice problems, or teach the material aloud.
Tests feel harder than rereading because they are — and that difficulty is precisely what builds the memory.

visual.text: Difficulty is the point, not the problem.

scenes.narration: Study rule: if you haven’t been tested on it, you don’t actually know it.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Compound Interest
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why starting early matters more than saving more.

scenes.narration: Compound interest means you earn interest on your interest. Small amounts grow surprisingly fast.
The formula: final amount equals principal times one plus rate, raised to the number of periods.
Example: $1,000 at 7 percent for 30 years. The answer isn’t $3,100 — it’s about $7,600.
Doubled the time, squared the growth. The exponential curve bends up sharply over decades.
This is why starting early matters more than saving more. A 20-year-old who saves $100 a month ends up with more than a 30-year-old who saves $200.

visual.text: Time compounds. Money that sits, grows.

scenes.narration: Compound interest is the most important equation in personal finance. Start early. Be patient.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Five-Paragraph Essay — And When to Break It
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing

scenes.narration: The five-paragraph essay is training wheels: intro, three body paragraphs, conclusion. Simple, reliable.
The intro states the thesis and previews three supporting arguments.
Each body paragraph makes one of those arguments with evidence. One point. One paragraph. No mixing.

visual.text: Body: one claim per paragraph + evidence

scenes.narration: The conclusion restates the thesis, synthesizes the arguments, and offers a final thought — not new evidence.
Real writing breaks this mold. Arguments may need one paragraph or seven. Evidence may deserve its own chapter.

visual.text: Beyond school: vary length to fit the argument

scenes.narration: Start with five. Graduate from it. The rule is: structure should serve the argument, not the other way around.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Decimals as Fractions
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why 0.25 means one quarter, and what place value really is.

scenes.narration: A decimal is a shorthand for a fraction whose denominator is a power of ten.
0.1 means one-tenth. 0.01 means one-hundredth. 0.001 means one-thousandth.
0.25 is the same as 25 hundredths, which reduces to one quarter.
Every repeating decimal is a rational number. For example, 0.333… equals one third.
Non-repeating, non-terminating decimals — like pi — are irrational.
Remember: a decimal is just a fraction wearing a disguise.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Percentages: Parts of 100
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Converting between percents, decimals, and fractions.

scenes.narration: Percent means "per hundred." Fifty percent is fifty out of every hundred.
To convert a percent to a decimal, move the point two places left. 75% becomes 0.75.
To find a percent of a number, multiply. 20% of 50 equals 0.20 times 50, which is 10.
Percent change is the difference over the original, times 100.
A 20% discount followed by a 20% markup does NOT return to the original. It ends 4% lower.
Percents are ratios. Convert to fractions and the logic becomes obvious.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Ratios and Proportions
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Ratios compare quantities; proportions equate two ratios.

scenes.narration: A ratio compares two quantities. Three cats for every two dogs is 3 to 2.
Ratios can be written three ways: with a colon, as a fraction, or with "to".
A proportion says two ratios are equal. Cross-multiply to solve.
Scale models, unit conversions, and cooking recipes all use proportions.

visual.text: If 2 cups serves 3, then 4 cups serves 6.

scenes.narration: Master ratios and you master rates, speed, density, and many real-world problems.
An exponent counts how many times a base multiplies itself.
When multiplying powers of the same base, add exponents.
When dividing, subtract exponents.
Raising a power to a power? Multiply the exponents.
Anything to the zero is one. Negative exponents flip to reciprocals.
Three rules, everywhere. Memorize by practice, not by rote.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Radicals and Roots
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Square roots, cube roots, and how to simplify them.

scenes.narration: The square root of a number is what you multiply by itself to get it.
The square root of 9 is 3 because 3 squared is 9.
To simplify, pull out perfect square factors.
Radicals can be written as fractional exponents. The square root equals the one-half power.
Rationalize denominators by multiplying by the radical.
Treat radicals like exponents, and the rules become familiar.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Scientific Notation
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: A × 10^n: writing huge and tiny numbers compactly.

scenes.narration: Scientific notation writes numbers as a coefficient times a power of ten.
300,000,000 becomes 3 times 10 to the 8th. The exponent counts decimal places moved.
Tiny numbers get negative exponents. One-millionth is ten to the negative six.
To multiply, multiply coefficients and add exponents.
Used in physics, chemistry, astronomy — anywhere numbers span many orders of magnitude.
Scientific notation: compression for numbers.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Solving Inequalities
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Same rules as equations, with one catch.

scenes.narration: An inequality compares two expressions using less than, greater than, or equal symbols.
Solve them like equations — add, subtract, multiply, divide on both sides.
ONE catch: when you multiply or divide by a negative, FLIP the inequality.
Solutions are ranges, not points. Graph them on a number line.
Compound inequalities combine with "and" or "or".
Inequalities describe everything from speed limits to optimization.
Without conventions, 2 + 3 × 4 could be 20 or 14. Order of operations fixes the answer.
The rule: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication and Division, Addition and Subtraction.
Multiplication and division share priority — do them left to right.
Same for addition and subtraction. Left to right.
Negative signs attach to the number. Powers of negatives need parentheses.
PEMDAS: simple hierarchy, removes ambiguity.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Polynomials: Add, Subtract, Multiply
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Combining like terms and the FOIL method.

scenes.narration: A polynomial is a sum of terms with whole-number exponents on the variable.
Like terms have the same variable and exponent. Combine by adding coefficients.
To multiply binomials, use FOIL: First, Outer, Inner, Last.
Simplify by combining like terms.
Polynomials appear in physics, economics, graphics, and signal processing.

visual.text: f(x) = ax² + bx + c models many curves

scenes.narration: FOIL is the workhorse of algebra.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Quadratic Vertex Form
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: y = a(x − h)² + k and completing the square.

scenes.narration: Vertex form reveals a parabola's peak or trough at a glance.
The vertex is at (h, k). The sign of a flips the parabola up or down.
Convert standard form by completing the square.
Example: x² + 6x + 5 becomes (x+3)² − 4. Vertex at (−3, −4).
Vertex form is best for graphing. Standard form is best for factoring.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: What Is a Function?
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Input → rule → output. One output per input.

scenes.narration: A function is a rule that assigns one output to each input.
We write f(x) = 2x + 3. The f is the name, x is the input, 2x + 3 is the rule.
To evaluate, substitute. f(4) equals 2 times 4 plus 3, which is 11.
The vertical line test: if any vertical line crosses the graph twice, it is not a function.
Domain is allowed inputs. Range is possible outputs.
Everything in calculus, stats, and CS traces back to functions.
Function composition chains two functions: apply g, then f.
Notation: f(g(x)) means plug g(x) into f.
Example: f(x) = x + 1, g(x) = 2x. Then f(g(3)) = f(6) = 7.
Order matters. f(g(x)) is usually different from g(f(x)).
Composition underlies chain rule in calculus and function pipelines in programming.
An inverse function undoes the original. If f takes 3 to 7, f-inverse takes 7 to 3.
To find f-inverse, swap x and y, then solve for y.
Example: y = 2x + 3. Swap: x = 2y + 3. Solve: y = (x − 3)/2.
A function has an inverse only if it is one-to-one. Horizontal line test.

visual.text: f(x) = x² has no inverse on all reals

scenes.narration: Inverses are reflections across the line y = x.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Unit Circle
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: How sine and cosine live on a circle of radius one.

scenes.narration: The unit circle has radius 1, centered at the origin.
Any point on it can be written as (cos θ, sin θ), where θ is the angle from the positive x-axis.
At 0 degrees, the point is (1, 0). At 90 degrees, it is (0, 1). At 180, it is (−1, 0).
Because the point is on a circle of radius 1, sin squared plus cos squared equals 1.
This single identity generates dozens of trig relationships.
A degree divides a full circle into 360 parts — a convention from ancient astronomy.
A radian is the angle subtended by an arc equal to the radius. 2π radians is a full circle.
Convert degrees to radians by multiplying by π over 180.
Radians make calculus clean: the derivative of sin x is cos x only if x is in radians.
In software, trig functions always expect radians. Remember to convert from degrees.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Arithmetic and Geometric Sequences
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Two patterns: add a constant, or multiply a ratio.

scenes.narration: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers. Patterns reveal themselves.
Arithmetic sequences add a common difference each step.
Geometric sequences multiply by a common ratio each step.
Sum an arithmetic series with n times the average of first and last.
Sum a geometric series: a over one minus r, if r is between −1 and 1.
Arithmetic grows linearly. Geometric grows exponentially. Huge difference at scale.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Limits at Infinity
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: What happens to a function as x grows without bound.

scenes.narration: A limit at infinity asks: what value does the function approach as x grows huge?
For rational functions, compare the degrees of top and bottom.
Top degree bigger? Limit is infinity. Bottom bigger? Limit is zero.
Equal degrees? The limit is the ratio of leading coefficients.
These are the end-behavior asymptotes of the graph.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Chain Rule
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Differentiating a function of a function.

scenes.narration: The chain rule differentiates composite functions.
In words: the derivative of the outer function times the derivative of the inner.
Example: differentiate sin(x²). Outer is sine, inner is x². Result: cos(x²) times 2x.
Chain rule stacks. Three nested functions? Multiply three derivatives.
Backpropagation in neural networks is just chain rule at massive scale.
Outer times inner. Always.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Definite Integral
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Area under the curve, summed from a to b.

scenes.narration: A definite integral is the signed area under a curve between two x-values.
Riemann sums approximate it with thin rectangles. As width shrinks, the sum becomes exact.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects integration to differentiation.
F is any antiderivative of f. Integration is the reverse of differentiation.

visual.text: If F'(x) = f(x), then ∫f dx = F + C

scenes.narration: Use definite integrals for area, distance from velocity, total accumulation.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: u-Substitution
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Reversing the chain rule for integrals.

scenes.narration: u-substitution simplifies an integral by renaming part of it.
Pick u to be an inner function whose derivative is also present.
Let u = x². Then du = 2x dx. The integral becomes cos(u) du.
Substitute back: sin(x²) + C.
u-sub is the inverse of the chain rule. Spot the pattern: f(g(x)) · g'(x).

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Vectors: Magnitude and Direction
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: More than a number — a quantity with direction.

scenes.narration: A vector has both magnitude and direction. Forces, velocities, and displacements are all vectors.
Add vectors tip to tail, or add components.
Magnitude is the Pythagorean length of the components.
Scalar multiplication stretches without changing direction.
Vectors are the language of physics, graphics, and machine learning.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Dot Product
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Multiplying two vectors into a scalar.

scenes.narration: The dot product of two vectors is the sum of component-wise products.
For vectors a and b, a·b equals a₁b₁ + a₂b₂.
It also equals the magnitudes times cos θ, where θ is the angle between them.
If the dot product is zero, the vectors are perpendicular.
Used in lighting calculations, physics work, and cosine similarity in ML.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Matrix Multiplication
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Row times column, one entry at a time.

scenes.narration: A matrix is a rectangle of numbers. Multiplying them combines linear transformations.
To multiply A times B, A must have as many columns as B has rows.

visual.text: A: m×n   B: n×p   →   AB: m×p

scenes.narration: Each entry of AB is row of A dotted with column of B.
Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative. AB usually differs from BA.
Foundation of linear algebra, 3D graphics, and machine learning.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Atoms, in 90 Seconds
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Protons, neutrons, electrons — the three-particle model.

scenes.narration: Everything is made of atoms. An atom has a tiny nucleus surrounded by a cloud of electrons.
The nucleus contains protons (positive) and neutrons (neutral).
Electrons (negative) orbit the nucleus. Atoms are mostly empty space.
Number of protons defines the element. Carbon has 6, oxygen has 8.
Change the neutrons → isotope. Change the electrons → ion.
Atoms: three particles, infinite complexity.
Atoms bond to achieve stable electron shells.
In an ionic bond, one atom donates electrons to another.
Ions attract oppositely, forming crystals. Salt is ionic.
In a covalent bond, atoms share electrons.
Ionic compounds dissolve in water. Covalent compounds often don't.
Ionic = transfer. Covalent = share. Simple, but predicts most of chemistry.
Atoms are too small to count individually. Chemists use moles.
One mole is 6.022 times 10 to the 23rd of particles. Avogadro's number.
The molar mass of a compound (in grams) equals the atomic mass sum (in amu).
To convert: mass over molar mass equals moles.
Moles let you count atoms by weighing them.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Ideal Gas Law
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: PV = nRT: pressure, volume, moles, temperature.

scenes.narration: An ideal gas follows a simple relationship: PV equals nRT.
P is pressure, V is volume, n is moles, R is the gas constant, T is absolute temperature.
Hold n and T constant: PV is constant. That is Boyle's law.
Hold P and n constant: V is proportional to T. That is Charles' law.
Always use absolute temperature in Kelvin, never Celsius.
Real gases deviate at high pressure or low temperature.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Ohm's Law
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: V = IR: voltage equals current times resistance.

scenes.narration: Ohm's law links voltage, current, and resistance in a circuit.
Voltage pushes charge. Current is the flow. Resistance opposes.

visual.text: V: volts   I: amps   R: ohms

scenes.narration: Double the voltage, double the current — if resistance is fixed.
Power dissipated equals voltage times current, or I squared R.
Ohm's law only holds for "ohmic" materials — not all circuits obey.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Wave Basics
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Wavelength, frequency, and the wave equation.

scenes.narration: A wave transfers energy without transferring matter.
Wavelength is peak-to-peak distance. Frequency is cycles per second.
Wave speed equals wavelength times frequency.
Light speed is 3 times 10 to the 8 meters per second. Frequency and wavelength trade off.
Longitudinal waves compress along motion. Transverse waves perpendicular.
All of electromagnetism and quantum mechanics rides on waves.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Work and Energy
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Work transfers energy. Energy is conserved.

scenes.narration: Work is force times distance, in the direction of the force.
Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.
Potential energy from gravity: mgh, where h is height.
Energy is conserved. Energy in one form becomes another.
Work-energy theorem: net work equals change in kinetic energy.
Energy can change form but never disappears.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Momentum and Impulse
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Mass in motion, and what changes it.

scenes.narration: Momentum is mass times velocity. It has direction.
Impulse is force times time, and it changes momentum.
In a collision, total momentum of the system is conserved.
Elastic collisions also conserve kinetic energy. Inelastic do not.

visual.text: Billiards: nearly elastic   Car crash: inelastic

scenes.narration: Seat belts extend the time of a collision, reducing force.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Circular Motion
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Why you accelerate even at constant speed.

scenes.narration: Moving in a circle means your direction keeps changing — so you are accelerating.
Centripetal acceleration points toward the center. Its magnitude is v² over r.
The force creating this is centripetal force: mass times centripetal acceleration.
Centrifugal force is fictitious — it only appears in the rotating frame.
Orbits, car turns, merry-go-rounds — all circular dynamics.
A conservation law says a quantity doesn't change over time in a closed system.
Energy is conserved. It can transform, but total stays constant.
Momentum is conserved in collisions and explosions, if no outside force.
Charge is conserved. Net electric charge before equals net charge after.
Noether's theorem: every continuous symmetry yields a conservation law.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Mitosis: Cell Division
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: One cell becomes two identical copies.

scenes.narration: Mitosis is how a cell divides into two genetically identical daughters.
First, DNA doubles during interphase.
Then four phases: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase.
Chromosomes line up at the middle, then get pulled to opposite poles.
Cytokinesis splits the cytoplasm, yielding two new cells.
Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism for evolution.
First: individuals in a population vary.
Second: some variations are heritable — passed to offspring.
Third: some variations affect survival or reproduction.
Over generations, favorable traits accumulate. The population evolves.
No intent. No goal. Just consequence.
Gregor Mendel crossed pea plants and found predictable inheritance patterns.
Each gene has two alleles. One from each parent.
Dominant alleles mask recessive ones when paired.
Crossing Aa × Aa gives AA : Aa : aa in 1 : 2 : 1 ratio — or 3 : 1 phenotypes.
Use Punnett squares to predict offspring probabilities.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Plate Tectonics
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: How continents move and why earthquakes happen.

scenes.narration: Earth's crust is broken into plates that slowly move on the mantle below.
Three boundary types: divergent, convergent, and transform.
Divergent: plates move apart, creating new crust. Mid-ocean ridges.
Convergent: plates collide. Mountains rise; subduction zones form trenches and volcanoes.
Transform: plates slide past. Earthquakes along faults like San Andreas.
Continents are in slow, constant motion — about as fast as fingernails grow.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Solar System
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: One star, eight planets, a lot of empty space.

scenes.narration: The Solar System is our star and everything bound to it gravitationally.
Four rocky inner planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars.
Four giant outer planets: Jupiter and Saturn (gas), Uranus and Neptune (ice).
Asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter. Kuiper belt past Neptune.
Pluto is a dwarf planet. The Oort Cloud bounds it all.
And 99.86% of the system's mass is the Sun.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Variables and Types
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: A name, a value, and a kind.

scenes.narration: A variable is a named container for a value.
In most languages, values have types: number, string, boolean, list.
Static typing fixes the type at declaration. Dynamic typing infers at runtime.
Type errors catch bugs. Know your types.
Variables are the atoms of every program.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Loops
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Repeating code until a condition fails.

scenes.narration: A loop repeats a block of code.
A for-loop runs a known number of times.
A while-loop runs until a condition is false.
Infinite loops are a bug. Always ensure your condition can eventually fail.
Nested loops multiply work. Watch out for quadratic blowup.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Arrays
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: An ordered list of values, indexed from zero.

scenes.narration: An array is a list of values stored in order.
Indices usually start at zero. First element is arr[0].
Access by index is O(1). Search for a value is O(n).
Many algorithms depend on arrays: sorting, searching, dynamic programming.

visual.text: Merge sort, binary search, DP tables

scenes.narration: Think of arrays as numbered pigeonholes.
Object-oriented programming bundles data and behavior into objects.
A class is a template. An object is an instance of a class.

visual.text: class Dog { ... }   let rex = new Dog()

scenes.narration: Encapsulation hides internal state behind methods.
Inheritance lets a subclass reuse and extend a parent class.
Polymorphism: one interface, many implementations.
OOP organizes large codebases. Used throughout Java, C++, Python.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Binary Search
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Finding an item in log(n) time.

scenes.narration: Binary search finds an item in a sorted list in logarithmic time.
Check the middle. If too small, discard left half. If too big, discard right.

visual.text: Halve the search space each step

scenes.narration: Each step halves the list. For n items, log₂(n) steps max.
A million items need only 20 comparisons.
Requires sorted input. Linear search works on any list.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Hash Tables
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: O(1) lookup by key, most of the time.

scenes.narration: A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function.
The hash function converts a key into an array index.
Lookup, insert, delete — all average O(1).
Collisions happen. Chaining or probing resolves them.
Dictionaries, maps, sets, caches — all hash tables under the hood.
A state machine is a system in one of several states, with transitions between them.
Traffic light: states are red, yellow, green. Transitions are time-based.
Inputs trigger transitions. Each state can produce outputs.
Used in: UI workflows, parsers, game AI, network protocols.
State machines make complex control flow explicit and testable.
Version control tracks every change to your code.
Git stores snapshots (commits) of your project over time.
Branches let you work on features in parallel.
Merging combines changes. Conflicts happen when two edits touch the same lines.
Distributed: every developer has the full history.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Encryption Basics
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Scrambling data so only the recipient can read it.

scenes.narration: Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext using a key.
Symmetric encryption: same key to encrypt and decrypt.
Asymmetric: public key to encrypt, private key to decrypt.
HTTPS uses asymmetric to exchange a symmetric key, then encrypts traffic.
Encryption protects your bank data, messages, medical records.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Topic Sentences
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: One sentence that previews a paragraph.

scenes.narration: A topic sentence announces the paragraph's main idea in one line.
It usually comes first. Readers know what to expect.
Specific, but not narrow. Opinion, not bare fact.

visual.text: Bad: "Dogs exist."   Good: "Dogs changed human society."

scenes.narration: Every following sentence should support the topic sentence.
A paper without topic sentences is a paper without a spine.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Writing Transitions
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Guiding the reader from one idea to the next.

scenes.narration: Transitions tell readers how ideas relate.
Addition: also, moreover, furthermore. Contrast: however, but, yet.
Cause/effect: therefore, thus, as a result. Time: next, before, finally.
Transitions can be phrases or whole sentences bridging two paragraphs.

visual.text: "Having shown X, we now examine Y."

scenes.narration: Without transitions, writing reads like a list.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Metaphor, Simile, Symbolism
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Three tools that make prose memorable.

scenes.narration: Metaphor says one thing is another: "time is money."
Simile is a softer metaphor using "like" or "as": "sharp as a knife."
Symbolism: one thing represents another. A rose means love. A dove means peace.
Use sparingly. Too many metaphors overload the reader.

visual.text: One strong image > ten weak ones

scenes.narration: Good writing makes you feel. Figurative language is how.
There are four main modes of writing.
Narrative tells a story with characters and events.
Descriptive paints a picture: sensory details.
Expository explains or informs: essays, articles, manuals.
Persuasive argues a position, tries to change your mind.
Most real writing blends modes. Know the mix you're aiming for.
Citations tell readers where your information came from.
MLA is common in humanities. Author-page format.
APA is common in social sciences. Author-year format.
End with a Works Cited (MLA) or References (APA) page with full bibliographic info.
Citing is ethical. Plagiarism is theft.
First drafts are rarely good. Great writing emerges from revision.
Read your work out loud. Awkward sentences jump out.
Cut. Most drafts are 20% too long.
Replace weak verbs. "Is" and "are" should not be everywhere.
Check each paragraph has a clear topic. Check each sentence earns its place.
Sleep on it. Return with fresh eyes.
Pomodoro is a productivity technique using timed focus intervals.
Work 25 minutes. Break 5. After four cycles, take a longer break.
During focus, no distractions: phone off, one task.
During breaks, move, stretch, rest your eyes. Do not check social media.
Short, bounded effort beats endless grinding.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: The Feynman Technique
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it.

scenes.narration: Richard Feynman's method for learning: explain, find gaps, fix, repeat.
Step one: pick a topic and pretend to teach it to a child.
Step two: notice where you got stuck or used jargon.
Step three: go back to your source and fill in those gaps.
Step four: simplify again. Repeat until it flows.
Teaching is the fastest test of real understanding.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Cornell Note-Taking
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: A three-zone page for better retention.

scenes.narration: Cornell notes divide a page into three zones: notes, cues, summary.
Right column: main notes during the lecture.
Left column: questions or keywords, filled in later.
Bottom: summary in your own words, written after class.
To study: cover the right side and try to recall from the cues.
Structure forces processing. Better than copying slides.
Test anxiety is a stress response. Your body, not your brain, starts it.
Box breathing: inhale four, hold four, exhale four, hold four. Repeat.
Scan the whole test first. Start with the easy questions.
If stuck, skip and return. Do not freeze.
Sleep and prior preparation reduce anxiety. There is no last-minute cure.
Calm the body. The mind follows.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Interleaved Practice
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Mix topics instead of massing one.

scenes.narration: Most students mass-practice: do all the algebra, then all the geometry.
Interleaving instead mixes topics in each session.
Performance during practice drops. Performance on the test rises.
Your brain learns to choose the right approach, not just apply one.
Feels less productive. Is more productive.
A fixed mindset believes intelligence is static. A growth mindset believes it can grow.
With a fixed mindset, failure feels like proof of limits.
With a growth mindset, failure is feedback.
Praise effort, not inherent smartness. Effort compounds.
Skill grows with deliberate, challenging practice.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Time Blocking
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Schedule your tasks into calendar blocks.

scenes.narration: To-do lists prioritize tasks. Time blocking places them on a calendar.
For each important task, assign it a specific block of time.
You face reality: can 12 tasks fit in 8 hours? No.
Include breaks, commute, and buffer. Calendar the whole day.
Commitment to blocks > commitment to lists.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Mindfulness for Focus
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Two minutes of breath-awareness resets focus.

scenes.narration: Mindfulness is paying attention to the present moment without judgment.
Sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Notice your breath coming in and going out.
Your mind will wander. That is normal. When it does, return attention to the breath.
Two minutes before studying lowers heart rate, improves concentration.
Not about emptying your mind. About noticing it.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Deliberate Practice
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Practice targets a weak spot. Not mindless repetition.

scenes.narration: Ordinary practice repeats what you already know. Deliberate practice targets what you don't.
First: identify a specific weakness.
Second: design a drill that targets exactly that weakness.
Third: get immediate feedback. Adjust.
Ten minutes of deliberate practice > one hour of casual practice.
Effort and feedback, focused on weakness, compound into skill.
Your brain replays the day during sleep, strengthening recent memories.
Deep NREM sleep consolidates facts. REM sleep consolidates skills.
Cramming cuts sleep. Cutting sleep hurts retention. Counterproductive.
Even a 20-minute nap after studying helps consolidation.
If you can only change one study habit, sleep more.
Before citing a source, evaluate it. Bad sources poison good research.
Peer-reviewed journals: highest quality. Slow, rigorous, expert reviewed.
Reputable news: solid journalism. Verify with multiple sources.
Wikipedia: good starting point. Check the references.
Blogs, social media, AI outputs: unverified. Always double-check.
Ask: who wrote it, when, why, and what's their evidence?

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Climate vs Weather
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Weather is the mood. Climate is the personality.

scenes.narration: Weather is what's happening now. Climate is the long-term pattern.
A cold snap next week: weather. Average temperatures over decades: climate.
Weather can vary wildly day to day. Climate changes slowly over years.
A cold day does not disprove climate change. A hot day does not confirm it.
Weather is the mood today. Climate is the personality over a lifetime.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Visualizing Recursion
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Function calls stack like Russian nesting dolls.

scenes.narration: A recursive function calls itself. Each call creates a new frame on the stack.
Think of stacking dolls: each doll contains a smaller one, until the smallest.
The deepest call — the base case — returns first. Then each caller resolves in reverse.
Without a base case, stack overflows. Always ensure a terminating condition.
Recursion solves tree traversal, parsers, search, and divide-and-conquer problems.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: BFS and DFS
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Two ways to explore a graph.

scenes.narration: Graphs have nodes connected by edges. Traversal visits every node.
BFS — breadth-first search — explores neighbors level by level. Uses a queue.
DFS — depth-first search — goes deep before backtracking. Uses a stack.
BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
DFS is good for cycle detection, topological sort.
Same idea, different container. Wide or deep.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Eisenhower Matrix
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Sort tasks by urgency and importance.

scenes.narration: Most of your tasks are urgent or important — but not both.
Quadrant 1: Urgent + Important. Do now.
Quadrant 2: Important, not Urgent. Schedule.
Quadrant 3: Urgent, not Important. Delegate.
Quadrant 4: Not Urgent, not Important. Delete.
Live in Q2 when possible. Prevent Q1.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Integers and Absolute Value
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Negative numbers and distance from zero.

scenes.narration: Integers include all whole numbers and their negatives.
The absolute value is distance from zero, always non-negative.
Adding a negative is subtracting: 3 + (−5) equals −2.
Multiplying two negatives gives a positive.
Absolute value turns any number positive.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Prime Factorization
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Every integer breaks into primes uniquely.

scenes.narration: A prime is a whole number greater than 1 with only 1 and itself as divisors.
Every integer > 1 factors uniquely into primes.
Use a factor tree. Break 60 into 6 × 10, then 2 × 3 × 2 × 5.
Prime factorization is used for GCF, LCM, and cryptography.
Unique factorization is a foundational theorem of number theory.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: GCF and LCM
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Greatest common factor and least common multiple.

scenes.narration: The GCF is the largest number that divides both.
The LCM is the smallest multiple shared by both.
Use prime factorization. GCF: common primes, minimum power. LCM: all primes, maximum power.

visual.text: GCF: min powers. LCM: max powers.

scenes.narration: A useful identity: GCF times LCM equals the product of the two numbers.
GCF reduces fractions. LCM finds common denominators.
To solve |x| = 5, consider both cases: x = 5 or x = −5.
For |x − 3| = 7, we get x − 3 = 7 or x − 3 = −7.
Always isolate the absolute value first. If |x| = negative, no solution exists.
For inequalities: |x| < a means −a < x < a. |x| > a means x < −a or x > a.
Absolute value measures distance. Equations say "distance equals...".
Direct variation: as x grows, y grows in proportion.
Inverse variation: as x grows, y shrinks.
For direct: graph is a line through the origin.
For inverse: graph is a hyperbola.
Speed × time = distance: direct in either factor. Pressure × volume = constant: inverse.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Slope-Intercept Form
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: y = mx + b: slope and y-intercept visible.

scenes.narration: Slope-intercept form is y = mx + b.
m is the slope. b is the y-intercept.
To graph, plot (0, b), then use slope to find another point.
From two points, compute m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁), then solve for b.
Most convenient form for graphing and identifying behavior.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Point-Slope Form
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) when you know a point and slope.

scenes.narration: Point-slope form writes a line using one point and the slope.
Example: slope 3 through (2, 5). y − 5 = 3(x − 2).
Easy to derive from two points or point plus slope.
Expand to get slope-intercept, or leave as-is for elegance.
Three forms of a line: point-slope, slope-intercept, standard. Know all three.
Parallel lines never meet. They have the same slope.
Perpendicular lines cross at right angles. Their slopes are negative reciprocals.
Example: if one line has slope 2, a perpendicular has slope −1/2.
Horizontal and vertical are perpendicular: slopes 0 and undefined.
Find equations of parallel or perpendicular lines by matching or flipping slope.
Distance between two points uses the Pythagorean theorem.
Midpoint averages coordinates.
Example: (1, 2) and (4, 6). Distance: √(9 + 16) = 5. Midpoint: (2.5, 4).
These formulas extend to 3D by adding a z-term.
Foundation of coordinate geometry.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Special Factoring Patterns
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Difference of squares, perfect squares, sum of cubes.

scenes.narration: Three patterns appear constantly: difference of squares, perfect square trinomials, sum or difference of cubes.
Difference of squares: a² − b² = (a − b)(a + b).
Perfect square: a² + 2ab + b² = (a + b)².
Sum of cubes: a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²).
Recognizing patterns saves massive time on algebra 2 and calculus.
A rational exponent is a fraction. It combines powers and roots.
x^(1/2) is the square root. x^(1/3) is the cube root.
x^(2/3) equals the cube root of x squared, or the cube root squared.
Rational exponents let you use all power rules on radicals.
Rewriting as fractional exponents unlocks problems.
Exponential growth: quantity multiplied by a factor each unit of time.
If k > 0, growth. If k < 0, decay.
Population doubles every τ: y = y₀ · 2^(t/τ).
Half-life: time to halve. y = y₀ · (1/2)^(t/h).
Carbon-14 dating, compound interest, viral spread all use this model.
Two triangles are congruent if corresponding sides and angles all match.
SSS: three sides equal → congruent.
SAS: two sides and included angle equal → congruent.
ASA: two angles and included side equal.
AAS: two angles and a non-included side. Also valid.
SSA is NOT valid in general — the ambiguous case.
Similar triangles have identical angles and proportional sides.
Angle-Angle (AA): if two angles match, triangles are similar.
Corresponding sides are in the same ratio: the scale factor.
Use similarity to find unknown lengths from a known pair.
All trigonometry comes from similar right triangles.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: SOH-CAH-TOA
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Sine, cosine, tangent in right triangles.

scenes.narration: In a right triangle, the three trig ratios relate sides to angles.
Sine = opposite / hypotenuse.
Cosine = adjacent / hypotenuse.
Tangent = opposite / adjacent.
Use to find missing sides or angles in any right triangle.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Law of Sines
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Not just right triangles — any triangle.

scenes.narration: The Law of Sines works for any triangle, not just right.
Side over sine of opposite angle is constant.
Use when you know two angles and one side (AAS/ASA), or two sides and a non-included angle (SSA).
SSA can have 0, 1, or 2 solutions — the ambiguous case.
Powerful tool for non-right triangles.
The Law of Cosines generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.
For any triangle: c² = a² + b² − 2ab·cos(C).
When C is 90°, cos is 0, and this reduces to the Pythagorean theorem.
Use for SAS (two sides and included angle) or SSS (find angles from sides).
Combines with Law of Sines to solve any triangle.
Pythagorean identity: sine squared plus cosine squared equals one.
Divide by cos² to get: tan² + 1 = sec².
Divide by sin² to get: 1 + cot² = csc².
These three identities simplify countless expressions.
Foundation for trig proofs and integral techniques.
The sine of a sum is not the sum of sines.
Sine formula: sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B.
Cosine formula: cos(A + B) = cos A cos B − sin A sin B.
Tangent: tan(A + B) = (tan A + tan B) / (1 − tan A tan B).
Double angle formulas come directly from setting A = B.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Polar Coordinates
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Distance and angle instead of x and y.

scenes.narration: Polar coordinates use a radius r and angle θ instead of (x, y).
Convert: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ.
Reverse: r = √(x² + y²), θ = arctan(y/x).
Circles, spirals, and rose curves are simpler in polar than rectangular.
Useful in physics (orbits), complex numbers, and integration.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Complex Numbers in Polar Form
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: r(cos θ + i sin θ) = re^(iθ).

scenes.narration: A complex number has a magnitude (distance from origin) and an angle.
Polar form: z = r(cos θ + i sin θ).
Euler's formula: that equals re^(iθ).
To multiply, multiply magnitudes and add angles.
De Moivre's theorem raises to a power: (re^(iθ))^n = r^n e^(inθ).
Dividing polynomials works like long division of integers.
Divide first term of numerator by first term of divisor.
Multiply result back and subtract.
Bring down the next term. Repeat until done.
Remainder goes over divisor as a fraction.
Synthetic division is a shortcut for dividing by x − k.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Remainder Theorem
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: P(k) is the remainder when dividing by x − k.

scenes.narration: When you divide P(x) by x − k, the remainder is simply P(k).
To check if k is a root: compute P(k). If 0, then (x − k) is a factor.
This is the Factor Theorem — a corollary of the Remainder Theorem.
Makes finding rational roots much faster than long division.
Combined with Rational Root Theorem, you can factor high-degree polynomials.
A rational function is a ratio of polynomials.
Vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator is zero (and numerator isn't).
Horizontal asymptotes depend on comparing degrees of top and bottom.
If top equals bottom, HA is the ratio of leading coefficients.
If top > bottom, there is a slant asymptote found by division.
Holes occur when a factor cancels in top and bottom.
A piecewise function uses different formulas for different intervals of x.
Each piece has a condition and a formula.
Absolute value is piecewise: x if non-negative, −x otherwise.
Graph each piece on its interval.
Tax brackets, shipping costs, and step functions are all piecewise.
A circle is the set of points at a fixed distance from a center.
Equation: (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r².
Center at (h, k). Radius r.
To find center and radius from general form, complete the square.

visual.text: Complete the square on x and y

scenes.narration: Comes from slicing a cone perpendicular to its axis.
An ellipse is like a stretched circle.
Equation: (x/a)² + (y/b)² = 1.
a and b are the semi-major and semi-minor axes.
Foci sit along the major axis. Sum of distances from any point to foci is constant.
Planetary orbits are ellipses (Kepler).
A hyperbola has two branches that open away from each other.
Equation: (x/a)² − (y/b)² = 1.
Has two foci. Difference of distances from any point to foci is constant.
Asymptotes: y = ±(b/a) x.
Open orbits and comet trajectories can be hyperbolic.
The Binomial Theorem expands (a + b)^n without multiplying n times.
(a + b)^n = Σ C(n,k) a^(n−k) b^k.
Coefficients come from Pascal's triangle or the formula n!/(k!(n−k)!).
Example: (a + b)⁴ = a⁴ + 4a³b + 6a²b² + 4ab³ + b⁴.
Used in probability, algebra, and approximation.
A permutation counts arrangements where order matters.
A combination counts selections where order doesn't.
Example: 10 runners, gold/silver/bronze medals → permutation.
10 people, pick 3 for a committee → combination.
Permutations equal combinations times k!.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Pigeonhole Principle
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: If n+1 items fit into n holes, one hole has ≥2.

scenes.narration: Pigeonhole principle: put n + 1 items into n boxes, one box gets at least 2.
Obvious once stated. Surprisingly powerful.
Example: 13 people, at least 2 share a birth month.
Used in proofs: Ramsey theory, number theory, CS.
If you squeeze more into less, some slot overflows.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Mathematical Induction
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Prove a statement for all integers.

scenes.narration: Induction proves a statement for all natural numbers.
Step 1: base case. Show it works for n = 1.
Step 2: inductive hypothesis. Assume it works for n = k.
Step 3: inductive step. Show it works for n = k + 1.
Like dominoes: the first falls, each one pushes the next.
Proves sum formulas, inequalities, divisibility.
Informally, a limit is what f approaches. Formally, the epsilon-delta definition.
Limit L exists if: for every ε > 0, there is a δ > 0 such that |x − c| < δ implies |f(x) − L| < ε.
Translation: get f as close to L as you want by choosing x close to c.
Proof-based calculus starts here.
Most of the time, limit laws spare us the epsilon-delta work.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Continuity
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: No gaps, no jumps, no holes.

scenes.narration: A function is continuous at c if: f(c) exists, lim f(x) exists, and they are equal.
Continuous functions you can draw without lifting your pen.
Polynomials are continuous everywhere. Rational functions, except where denominator is zero.
Continuity is a prerequisite for many theorems: IVT, MVT, extreme value.
If you can take the limit by plugging in, it's continuous there.
If f is continuous on [a, b] and f(a), f(b) have opposite signs, a root exists between.
Formally: f takes every value between f(a) and f(b).
Used to prove roots exist, even when you can't solve exactly.
Foundation of bisection method for numerical root-finding.
If it starts below zero and ends above, it crossed zero.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Mean Value Theorem
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Somewhere the slope equals the average.

scenes.narration: MVT: if f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c where f'(c) equals the average rate.
Intuition: your average speed over a trip equals your speedometer reading at some moment.
Special case: Rolle's theorem (when f(a) = f(b), some c has f'(c) = 0).
Used to prove uniqueness, inequalities, Taylor's theorem.
Somewhere, the slope equals the overall slope.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Optimization with Calculus
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Find max and min using derivatives.

scenes.narration: Optimization finds the largest or smallest value of a function.
Critical points where f'(x) = 0 or undefined are candidates.
Classify: second derivative test or sign chart.
Closed interval: also check endpoints.
Word problems: build the function, identify constraints, optimize.
How machine learning trains: find minima of loss functions.
Related rates: how one variable's change affects another's.
Write the geometric relationship first.
Differentiate both sides with respect to time.
Plug in known values and solve for the unknown rate.
Classic: ladder sliding down a wall.
Real application: radar, fluid flow, astronomy.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Linear Approximation
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Estimate a function using its tangent line.

scenes.narration: Near a point, a function looks linear. Use the tangent line to approximate.
Example: approximate √(4.1). f(x) = √x, a = 4. f'(4) = 1/4.
Actual: 2.0248. Very close.
Foundation for Newton's method, differentials, Taylor series.
Tangent = best linear approximation.
A Riemann sum approximates area under a curve by summing rectangle areas.
Split [a,b] into n equal pieces of width Δx.
Use left, right, or midpoint height for each rectangle.
Sum: Σ f(x_i) Δx.
As n → ∞, the Riemann sum becomes the integral.
Rectangles in the limit give exact area.
FTC part 1: the derivative of an integral returns the original function.
FTC part 2: to evaluate a definite integral, find an antiderivative and subtract.
Derivatives and integrals are inverse operations.
Finding antiderivative replaces summing infinite rectangles.
One of the most beautiful results in all of mathematics.
Integration by parts is the integral's version of the product rule.
Formula: ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du.
LIATE rule for choosing u: Log, Inverse, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential.
Example: ∫x e^x dx. u = x, dv = e^x dx. Result: x e^x − e^x + C.
Second most-used integration technique, after substitution.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Trigonometric Substitution
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Use trig identities to simplify integrals.

scenes.narration: Trig substitution swaps a variable for a trig function to exploit identities.
If integrand has √(a² − x²), let x = a sin θ.
If √(a² + x²), let x = a tan θ.
If √(x² − a²), let x = a sec θ.
Pythagorean identity turns the root into a single trig function.
A niche technique but handles tricky radicals.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Partial Fractions
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Break a rational function into simpler pieces.

scenes.narration: Partial fractions decompose a complex rational into simpler ones.
Clear denominators, match coefficients, solve for A, B.
Each piece integrates to a log. Reassemble.
Essential for integrating rational functions in calculus and differential equations.
A Taylor series represents a function as an infinite polynomial.
Taylor at a = 0 is called a Maclaurin series.
Example: e^x = 1 + x + x²/2 + x³/6 + ...
sin x = x − x³/6 + x⁵/120 − ...
Truncating gives polynomial approximations used by calculators, physics, ML.
Every smooth function becomes a polynomial in the limit.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Convergence Tests
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Does an infinite series add to something finite?

scenes.narration: Does a series converge or diverge? There are many tests.
Divergence test: if aₙ doesn't go to 0, series diverges.
p-series test: Σ 1/n^p converges iff p > 1.
Geometric series: converges iff |r| < 1.
Ratio test: limit |a_(n+1)/aₙ| < 1 means converge.
Integral test, comparison test, alternating series test also useful.
The cross product takes two 3D vectors and produces a third, perpendicular to both.
Magnitude: |a||b|sin θ. Direction: right-hand rule.
Zero when vectors are parallel (sin θ = 0).
Unlike dot product, cross product is anti-commutative: a × b = −(b × a).
Used for torque, angular momentum, surface normals.
The determinant of a square matrix is a number that encodes scaling and orientation.
2×2: ad − bc.
Zero determinant means the matrix is singular (not invertible).
Geometrically, absolute determinant is area (2D) or volume (3D) scaling.
3×3 computed by cofactor expansion or rule of Sarrus.

visual.text: A scalar that says a lot.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Directions that only scale under a matrix.

scenes.narration: An eigenvector is a nonzero vector whose direction is preserved by a matrix.
The scale factor λ is the eigenvalue.
Find them from det(A − λI) = 0.
Used in: PCA, quantum mechanics, PageRank, stability analysis.
Eigenvectors reveal a matrix's intrinsic directions.
Bayes' theorem flips conditional probabilities.
P(A) is prior. P(A|B) is posterior — after seeing B.
Classic example: disease test. Rare disease + 99% accurate test can have high false-positive rate.
Spam filters, medical diagnosis, and machine learning use Bayes.
Evidence updates belief — proportional to likelihood and prior.
A random variable is a function mapping outcomes to numbers.
Expected value is the probability-weighted average.
Variance measures spread: E[(X − μ)²].
Standard deviation is square root of variance.
The normal distribution is the bell curve defined by mean and variance.
Formula: (1/σ√(2π)) e^(-(x−μ)²/(2σ²)).
68-95-99.7 rule: that fraction lies within 1, 2, 3 standard deviations.
Arises from the Central Limit Theorem — sums of many small random effects.
Heights, test scores, measurement errors — all approximately normal.
Hypothesis testing evaluates whether data supports a claim.
Null hypothesis (H₀): no effect. Alternative (H₁): there is an effect.
Collect data. Compute a p-value: probability of seeing data this extreme if H₀ true.
If p < α (usually 0.05), reject H₀.
Not absolute truth — controls false positive rate at α.
Foundation of scientific statistical inference.
Correlation measures how two variables move together.
Pearson r is between −1 and 1.
1 = perfect positive. −1 = perfect negative. 0 = no linear relationship.
Linear regression finds the line y = ax + b that best fits the data.
Correlation is not causation. Two things can correlate for many reasons.
A set is a collection of distinct elements.
Union: all elements in either. A ∪ B.
Intersection: elements in both. A ∩ B.
Complement: not in A.
