De Morgan's laws: complement of a union is intersection of complements.
Foundation of modern mathematics, logic, and probability.
Propositions are statements that are true or false.
AND (∧) is true only when both are true.
OR (∨) is true when at least one is true.
NOT (¬) flips truth value.
Implication p → q is false only when p is true and q is false.
Foundation of mathematical proof and computer circuits.
Modular arithmetic wraps around after a fixed modulus.
Means: a and b differ by a multiple of n.
Add, subtract, multiply mod n just like normal arithmetic.
Used in: cryptography, computer science, number theory.
A clock is modular 12. Everything wraps.
A graph is a set of vertices connected by edges.
Undirected: edges have no direction. Directed: edges point.
Degree: number of edges at a vertex.
Paths, cycles, connectedness describe graph structure.
Used in: networks, social science, routing, scheduling.
Euler's Königsberg problem launched the whole field in 1736.
Each Fibonacci number is the sum of the previous two.
Ratio of consecutive terms approaches the golden ratio φ ≈ 1.618.
Appears in nature: flowers, pinecones, pineapples.
Closed form: Binet's formula involving φ.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Cell Structure
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: The basic parts of a cell.

scenes.narration: Cells are the basic unit of life. All organisms are made of cells.
Prokaryotic cells (bacteria) have no nucleus. Eukaryotic cells do.
Key organelles: nucleus (DNA), mitochondria (energy), ribosomes (protein), ER, Golgi, lysosomes.
Plant cells also have cell walls, chloroplasts, and large vacuoles.
Cell membrane controls what enters and leaves.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Cellular Respiration
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: How cells turn food into ATP.

scenes.narration: Cellular respiration converts glucose into ATP — cellular currency.
Three stages: glycolysis, Krebs cycle, electron transport chain.
Glycolysis: glucose splits into 2 pyruvate. Net 2 ATP.
Krebs cycle: pyruvate oxidizes, produces NADH and FADH₂.
Electron transport chain: carriers drive ATP synthesis. ~32 ATP.
Net: ~36-38 ATP per glucose. Oxygen is the final electron acceptor.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: DNA Replication
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Copying the genome before cell division.

scenes.narration: DNA replicates before every cell division. Semi-conservative.
Helicase unwinds the double helix.
DNA polymerase adds complementary nucleotides.
Leading strand continuous. Lagging strand in Okazaki fragments.
Ligase seals the gaps.
Result: two identical double helices. Errors are rare but happen.
The central dogma: DNA → RNA → protein.
Transcription: RNA polymerase reads DNA, makes mRNA.
Translation: ribosomes read mRNA in codons, build protein from amino acids.
Each codon (3 nucleotides) codes for one amino acid.
tRNA carries amino acids to the ribosome, matching anticodons to codons.
Result: a protein. All cellular function depends on this pipeline.
Every cell has the same DNA, but different cells use different genes.
Promoters are DNA regions where transcription starts.
Transcription factors bind DNA and recruit (or repress) RNA polymerase.
Epigenetic modifications (methylation, histone marks) silence or activate regions.
Regulation makes one genome into many cell types.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Allele frequencies stay stable without evolution.

scenes.narration: Hardy-Weinberg: in a non-evolving population, allele frequencies stay constant.
Assumptions: no mutation, no migration, large population, random mating, no selection.
p + q = 1 for two alleles.
Genotype frequencies: p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
If observed frequencies differ, something is driving evolution.
A null model for detecting selection, drift, or gene flow.
Speciation is the formation of a new species.
Allopatric: populations separated geographically diverge.
Sympatric: new species forms without physical separation (niche, polyploidy).
Reproductive isolation is the key step: individuals no longer interbreed.
Over time, accumulated differences make the new species distinct.
The immune system fights infection using two linked responses.
Innate: fast, non-specific. Skin, mucus, phagocytes, inflammation.
Adaptive: slow, specific. B cells make antibodies. T cells kill infected cells.
Memory cells remember pathogens, making future responses faster.
Vaccines train adaptive immunity without the disease.
Immune dysregulation leads to allergies, autoimmune disease, or immunodeficiency.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Food Webs
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Who eats whom in an ecosystem.

scenes.narration: A food web shows all the feeding relationships in an ecosystem.
Producers (plants) make their own food via photosynthesis.
Primary consumers eat producers. Secondary eat primary. And so on.
Energy transfers at ~10% efficiency per trophic level.
Decomposers recycle dead matter back to nutrients.
Remove a key species (keystone), the whole web shakes.
Biogeochemical cycles move matter between organisms and environment.
Carbon cycle: photosynthesis pulls CO₂ in, respiration puts it back.
Nitrogen cycle: bacteria fix atmospheric N₂ into usable forms.
Water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation.
Human activity (burning fossils, fertilizers) disrupts these cycles.
Populations grow exponentially when resources are unlimited.
Logistic growth: resources limit growth to carrying capacity K.
Near carrying capacity, growth slows to zero.
r-selected species: many offspring, fast growth (bacteria, insects).
K-selected species: few offspring, slow growth (elephants, humans).
Limits: food, space, disease, predation.
Electrons fill orbitals in a specific order.
Aufbau principle: fill lowest energy orbitals first.
Pauli exclusion: no two electrons have all same quantum numbers.
Hund's rule: spread across degenerate orbitals before pairing.
Valence electrons (outer shell) determine chemistry.
The periodic table is literally a map of electron configurations.
Atoms follow predictable trends across the periodic table.
Atomic radius: decreases across period, increases down group.
Ionization energy: energy to remove an electron. Increases right and up.
Electronegativity: tendency to attract electrons. Same direction as IE.
Periodic trends predict bonding behavior.
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs using dots and lines.
Count total valence electrons across all atoms.
Connect atoms with single bonds first.
Fill in lone pairs. Complete octets (except H: 2).
If short, form double or triple bonds.
Verify formal charges and total electron count.
VSEPR: electron pairs around a central atom repel each other.
2 groups: linear (180°). Example: CO₂.
3 groups: trigonal planar (120°). Example: BF₃.
4 groups: tetrahedral (109.5°). Example: CH₄.
5 groups: trigonal bipyramidal. 6: octahedral.
Lone pairs push harder than bonding pairs — distort angles.
Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules.
London dispersion: weakest. In all molecules. Temporary dipoles.
Dipole-dipole: polar molecules attract oppositely.
Hydrogen bonds: strongest IMF. H bonded to N, O, or F.
Stronger IMFs → higher boiling points.
Water's unique properties come from hydrogen bonding.
Bronsted acids donate protons (H+). Bases accept them.
Strong acids fully dissociate: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HClO₄.
Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by one proton.
pH = −log[H+]. Neutral water: pH 7.
Kw = [H+][OH-] = 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C.
Acids and bases neutralize to form salt and water.
A buffer resists changes in pH when acid or base is added.
Made of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or vice versa).
Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]).
Blood is buffered by bicarbonate — pH held near 7.4.
Buffers have limits. Too much added acid/base overwhelms them.
At equilibrium, the forward and reverse reaction rates are equal.
Concentrations don't change (but reactions continue).
Equilibrium constant Kc: ratio of products to reactants.
Large K: mostly products. Small K: mostly reactants.
Le Chatelier: disturb equilibrium, system shifts to counter the change.
Thermochemistry studies heat exchanged during reactions.
Exothermic: releases heat. ΔH < 0.
Endothermic: absorbs heat. ΔH > 0.
Hess's Law: total ΔH is path-independent.
Calorimetry measures heat using q = mcΔT.
Bond breaking absorbs energy. Bond forming releases.
Entropy S measures disorder. Universe tends toward more disorder.
Second law: entropy of an isolated system never decreases.
Gibbs free energy: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS.
ΔG < 0: spontaneous. ΔG > 0: non-spontaneous.
Temperature controls which term dominates.
Combines enthalpy (energy) and entropy (disorder) into one rule.
Electrochemistry links redox reactions and electricity.
Oxidation is loss of electrons. Reduction is gain. OIL RIG.
Galvanic cell: spontaneous redox produces electrical current.
Electrolytic cell: input current forces a non-spontaneous reaction.
Cell voltage from standard reduction potentials.
Batteries, fuel cells, corrosion, electroplating — all electrochemistry.
Organic molecules have functional groups that determine reactivity.
Alcohol: −OH. Methanol, ethanol.
Carboxylic acid: −COOH. Vinegar, acetic acid.
Amine: −NH₂. Related to ammonia. Amino acids.
Ester: −COO−. Fragrances, fats.
Ketone, aldehyde, ether, halide — each with characteristic behavior.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Projectile Motion
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Horizontal and vertical components are independent.

scenes.narration: A projectile has separate horizontal and vertical motion. They don't affect each other.
Horizontal: constant velocity (no air resistance).
Vertical: constant acceleration g downward.
Break initial velocity into v_x = v cos θ, v_y = v sin θ.
Range (on level ground): R = v² sin(2θ)/g. Max at θ = 45°.
Baseballs, arrows, cannonballs. Parabolic paths.
Rotational motion parallels linear, with angular quantities.
Angular velocity ω replaces v. Angular acceleration α replaces a.
Moment of inertia I replaces mass. Depends on shape.
Torque τ replaces force. τ = Iα.
Rotational kinetic energy: ½Iω².
Same laws, spinning instead of moving.
Torque is the rotational analog of force.
Magnitude: τ = rF sin θ.
Or: force times the perpendicular distance (lever arm).
Why long wrenches make loosening bolts easier.
Balance: counterclockwise torque equals clockwise torque.
Seesaws, doors, steering. Rotation requires torque.
Angular momentum is the rotational analog of linear momentum.
Conserved if no external torque acts.
Figure skater pulls arms in → I decreases → ω increases.
Planets sweep equal areas in equal times (Kepler's second law).
Key to orbits, gyroscopes, rotation stability.
Simple harmonic motion: oscillation with restoring force proportional to displacement.
Position: x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
Period for a spring: T = 2π√(m/k).
For a pendulum (small angle): T = 2π√(L/g).
Energy oscillates between kinetic and potential.
SHM models sound, waves, AC circuits, atoms.
The Doppler effect shifts observed frequency when source or observer moves.
Approaching source: higher frequency.
Receding source: lower frequency.
Formula: f' = f (v ± v_obs) / (v ∓ v_src).
Doppler radar, ambulance sirens, redshift of galaxies.
Redshift reveals universe expansion.
Coulomb's law: force between two charges.
k ≈ 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C². Charges in coulombs.
Same charges repel. Opposites attract.
Inverse-square dependence on distance.
Parallel to Newton's gravitational law in form.
The electric field at a point is the force a test charge would feel, per unit charge.
From a point charge: E = kQ/r².
Field lines point from + to −.
Field = gradient of electric potential.
Fields describe how charges interact without contact.
A capacitor stores energy in an electric field between two plates.
Capacitance: charge stored per volt applied.
Parallel plate: C = ε₀ A / d.
Energy stored: ½ CV².
Capacitors: power filters, memory cells, camera flashes.
Magnetic fields exert force on moving charges and currents.
Force on a moving charge: F = qv × B.
Force is perpendicular to velocity and field. Direction by right-hand rule.
Currents create magnetic fields (Ampere's law).
Changing magnetic field induces EMF (Faraday's law).
Motors, generators, transformers. Foundation of electricity grid.
Shine light on a metal: electrons ejected above a threshold frequency.
Below threshold, no electrons — no matter how intense the light.
Einstein: light comes in quanta (photons) of energy hf.
Max kinetic energy: KE = hf − work function.
Confirmed light's particle nature. Nobel Prize 1921.

visual.text: Light is both wave and particle

scenes.narration: Solar cells work on this principle.
Unstable nuclei decay, emitting radiation.
Alpha: emit helium nucleus. Beta: emit electron. Gamma: emit photon.
Half-life: time for half the sample to decay.
Carbon-14: half-life ~5700 years. Used for archaeological dating.
Uranium-238: half-life 4.5 billion years.
Exponential decay law governs all of it.
Rocks transform through three types in a cycle.
Igneous: formed from cooled magma or lava.
Sedimentary: compacted sediments layered over time.
Metamorphic: transformed by heat and pressure.
Weathering, deposition, heat, melting cycle them endlessly.
Earth's atmosphere has distinct layers with different properties.
Troposphere (0-12 km): weather happens here.
Stratosphere (12-50 km): ozone layer. Temperature rises with altitude.
Mesosphere: where meteors burn up.
Thermosphere: auroras, ISS orbit.
Exosphere: gradually merges into space.
Ocean currents transport heat around the globe.
Surface currents driven by winds.
Deep currents driven by density (temperature and salinity).
Gulf Stream warms Europe. El Niño disrupts Pacific patterns.
Ocean conveyor belt takes ~1000 years to circle globally.
Greenhouse gases trap infrared radiation from Earth's surface.
CO₂, methane, water vapor, N₂O are key greenhouse gases.
Human fossil fuel burning has raised CO₂ from 280 to 420+ ppm since 1850.
Earth has warmed ~1.1°C. Polar ice melts. Sea levels rise.
Effects: extreme weather, shifting biomes, ocean acidification.
Solutions: renewable energy, carbon removal, efficiency.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Life Cycle of a Star
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Nebula to neutron star or black hole.

scenes.narration: Stars form from collapsing clouds of gas and dust.
Core gets hot enough → fusion starts. Now it's a main-sequence star.
Low-mass stars: end as white dwarfs.
High-mass stars: explode as supernovae. Core becomes neutron star or black hole.
Supernovae forge heavy elements — iron and beyond.
Everything in you heavier than hydrogen came from dead stars.
If/else lets programs take different paths based on conditions.
Basic form: if (condition) { code }.
Add else for fallback.
Chain with else-if for multiple cases.
Nested if statements handle complex logic, but stay readable.
A function takes input, does work, returns output.
Parameters are placeholders for arguments passed in.
Return sends a value back to the caller.
Functions enable reuse: DRY — don't repeat yourself.
Pure functions have no side effects — same input, same output.
Scope is where a variable is accessible.
Global scope: accessible everywhere.
Function (local) scope: only inside the function.
Block scope (let, const): inside the block.
A closure captures variables from its enclosing scope.
Closures enable factories, private state, callbacks.
Arrow functions are a concise syntax for function expressions.
Higher-order functions take functions as arguments or return them.
map, filter, reduce — core functional toolkit.
Functions are values: assign to variables, pass around, return.
Core of functional programming and modern JS.
A stack is Last-In First-Out (LIFO).
Push adds to the top. Pop removes from the top.
Function call stack, undo history, backtracking.
A queue is First-In First-Out (FIFO).
Enqueue at back, dequeue at front.
Task queues, BFS, print queues.
A linked list is a sequence of nodes, each pointing to the next.
No contiguous memory. Grow and shrink freely.
Insert/delete at head: O(1).
Random access: O(n) — no indexing.
Variants: singly, doubly, circular.
Foundation for more advanced structures.
A tree is a hierarchical structure with a root and children.
Each node has zero or more child nodes. No cycles.
Binary tree: each node has at most 2 children.
Binary Search Tree: left subtree < node < right subtree.
File systems, DOM, parser ASTs are trees.
A heap is a complete binary tree with an ordering property.
Min-heap: parent ≤ children. Max-heap: parent ≥ children.
Insert and extract-min: both O(log n).
Priority queues built on heaps: always retrieve highest priority.
Used in Dijkstra, heapsort, event simulation.
Quicksort picks a pivot and partitions around it.
Elements smaller than pivot go left. Larger go right.
Recursively sort each partition.
Average case: O(n log n). Worst: O(n²) (bad pivot).

visual.text: Avg O(n log n), worst O(n²)

scenes.narration: Random pivot or median-of-three avoids worst case.
In-place and fast in practice. Most standard libraries use it.
Merge sort splits array in half, sorts each half, then merges.
Merging two sorted halves is O(n) per level.
Log n levels. Total: O(n log n).
Stable: preserves order of equal elements.
Needs O(n) extra space. Quicksort is in-place.
Great for linked lists (stable, no random access needed).
Dynamic programming solves problems with overlapping subproblems.
Store (memoize) results to avoid recomputing.
Top-down: recursion + memoization.
Bottom-up: iterative, fill a table.
Classic problems: Fibonacci, longest common subsequence, knapsack.
Turns exponential algorithms into polynomial ones.
A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step.
Sometimes this gives the global optimum. Sometimes not.
Works for: making change (with standard coins), Huffman coding, MST.
Fails for: knapsack, some graph problems.
Greedy is simple and fast — when it works.
Dijkstra finds shortest paths from a single source in a weighted graph.
Maintain tentative distances, starting at ∞ except source (0).
Repeatedly pick unvisited node with smallest distance.
Update its neighbors if a shorter path is found.
With priority queue: O((V + E) log V).
Only works for non-negative weights. Negative? Use Bellman-Ford.
HTTP methods define the type of request.
GET retrieves data. Safe and idempotent.
POST creates new resources. Not idempotent.
PUT replaces a resource. Idempotent.
DELETE removes. PATCH partial updates.
REST APIs use these verbs to map to CRUD operations.
SELECT is how you ask a database for data.
Basic: SELECT columns FROM table.
Filter with WHERE.
Sort with ORDER BY.
Limit with LIMIT.
Combine tables with JOIN. Foundation of all relational queries.
REST (Representational State Transfer): web service architectural style.
Resources identified by URLs. Actions via HTTP verbs.
Stateless: each request contains all info needed.
Typically returns JSON.
Status codes: 200 OK, 404 not found, 500 server error.
Most web APIs today are RESTful.
In supervised learning, the model learns from labeled data.
Input (x) mapped to output (y) — often called features and labels.
Classification predicts categories. Regression predicts numbers.
Algorithms: decision trees, SVM, neural nets, logistic regression.
Split data: train, validate, test.
Most useful ML today is supervised.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Neural Networks
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Layers of weighted sums and non-linearities.

scenes.narration: Neural networks are layered computational graphs inspired by the brain.
Input passes through weighted connections into neurons.
Each neuron applies a non-linear activation (ReLU, sigmoid).
Stack layers to build representations of increasing abstraction.
Train by gradient descent and backpropagation.
Today dominates vision, speech, language.
Gradient descent minimizes a loss function by stepping downhill.
Compute gradient of loss with respect to parameters.
Update: θ = θ − η ∇L.
η is the learning rate. Too big: overshoot. Too small: slow.
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) uses mini-batches.
Foundation of modern machine learning training.
A singular subject takes a singular verb. Plural subject, plural verb.
She runs. They run.
Don't let distance fool you. "The box of cookies is empty."
Either/or: verb matches the nearer subject.
Collective nouns (team, group) usually take singular.
Use commas to separate items in a list.
Use commas after introductory phrases.
Commas around nonessential info ("which" clauses).
Commas before coordinating conjunctions joining independent clauses.
Commas in direct address and after "yes/no".
Don't use a comma splice — two sentences joined only by a comma.
A semicolon joins two related independent clauses.
Or separates complex list items that contain commas.
A colon introduces a list, quote, or elaboration.
Before a colon, a complete sentence.
Use both sparingly for emphasis.
Active: subject acts. "The dog chased the cat."
Passive: subject receives the action. "The cat was chased by the dog."
Prefer active. Clearer, shorter, stronger.
Use passive when the actor is unknown or irrelevant.
Passive is useful — just don't overuse it.
Parallelism: items in a series share the same structure.
Bad: "I like running, to swim, and biking." Inconsistent.
Good: "I like running, swimming, and biking." All -ing.
Applies to verbs, phrases, clauses.
Parallel structure makes prose rhythmic and clear.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Misplaced Modifiers
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Who or what does the phrase modify?

scenes.narration: A modifier should clearly attach to the word it describes.
Bad: "Walking to school, a bird flew overhead."
Better: "While I was walking to school, a bird flew overhead."
Dangling modifiers are worse — no clear subject at all.
Read your sentences aloud. Attach modifiers to the right thing.
Aristotle identified three modes of persuasion.
Ethos: credibility. Why should we trust you?
Pathos: emotion. What do you make us feel?
Logos: logic. What does the evidence say?
Great persuasion balances all three.
Ad hominem: attacking the person instead of the argument.
Straw man: misrepresenting someone's argument to attack it.
Slippery slope: claiming one step leads to extreme outcome without evidence.
False dichotomy: only two options presented when more exist.
Appeal to authority: "X says so" without actual evidence.
Learn them to sharpen your own arguments and spot flawed ones.
A thesis is your essay's main argument in one sentence.
Specific: not "climate change is bad" but a specific claim.
Arguable: someone could reasonably disagree.
Supported: you can defend it with evidence.
Weak: "The Civil War had causes." Strong: "Economic divergence, not slavery alone, caused the Civil War."
Everything in your essay serves the thesis.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Paraphrasing and Summarizing
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Using sources in your own words.

scenes.narration: Paraphrasing restates a source in your own words, same length.
Summarizing compresses a source. Shorter than the original.
Both still need citations. You are using someone else's ideas.
Rule of thumb: change both words and sentence structure.
Failed paraphrase = plagiarism. Take care.
Studies consistently show: retrieval (self-testing) outperforms passive review.
Reading notes feels productive but is one of the least effective methods.
Close your notes. Try to recall. Check. Repeat.
Effortful recall signals the brain that info matters.
Flashcards work. Practice problems work. Explaining to yourself works.
Dual coding: combine verbal and visual information.
Draw diagrams for concepts you read about.
Annotate diagrams with explanations.
Your brain has separate verbal and visual systems. Engage both.
Sketchnotes, concept maps, mind maps are dual coding tools.
The method of loci associates items with locations you know well.
Imagine walking through your house. Place each item to remember at a location.
To recall, mentally retrace the walk.
Used by memory champions for decades.
Works because visual-spatial memory is extremely strong.
Mnemonics are memory shortcuts for hard-to-remember info.
Acronyms: HOMES for Great Lakes (Huron, Ontario, Michigan, Erie, Superior).
Rhymes: "In 1492, Columbus sailed the ocean blue."
Visual associations: "bear" for "bore" in a language.
Stories: chain items in a narrative. Brain loves stories.
Not just tricks — cognitive science endorses them.
Every notification costs ~20 minutes of focus recovery (studies).
Put your phone in another room. Not face-down. Another room.
Turn off notifications entirely during deep work.
Use focus apps (or just site blockers) if you need help.
Deep work is rare. Train the ability to focus.
Regular aerobic exercise boosts memory, focus, and mood.
Increases BDNF — a protein that grows new neurons.
Improves blood flow, delivering oxygen to the brain.
20-30 min of moderate cardio daily is enough.
Walking, running, swimming, biking. Pick what you'll actually do.
Goals alone rarely work. Implementation intentions do.
Format: "When X happens, I will do Y."
Example: "When I finish dinner, I will study for 30 min."
Studies show this roughly doubles follow-through.
Tie a habit to an existing trigger in your day.
SMART is an acronym for effective goal-setting.
Specific: "improve Spanish" → "learn 500 words".
Measurable: can you count progress?
Achievable: stretch, but reasonable.
Relevant: aligned with your larger aims.
Time-bound: "by June 1st".
Vague goals fail. SMART goals track progress.
Elaborative interrogation: ask "why is this true?" as you study.
Answering "why" forces active processing.
Connects new info to existing knowledge.
Example: "Why does water expand when it freezes?" → model hydrogen bonding.
Better than passively rereading. Makes knowledge stick.
Self-explanation: narrate your reasoning out loud or on paper.
"Step 1, I multiply both sides by 2 to eliminate the fraction..."
Forces you to make tacit knowledge explicit.
Gaps in your understanding become visible.
Similar to the Feynman technique, applied in the moment.
Sum of arithmetic series: pairs add to same total.
Works for any arithmetic sequence.
Derivation: write sum forward and backward, add.
Core formula for counting, finance, discrete math.
The discriminant determines nature of roots.
Positive: two distinct real roots.
Zero: one repeated real root.
Negative: no real roots (two complex).
Tells graph behavior before computing roots.
Compound inequalities combine two with "and" or "or".
AND: both must hold. Intersection of ranges.
OR: either holds. Union of ranges.
Graph: AND uses closed region; OR uses two disjoint regions.
Useful for constraints like "between a and b".
f(x) + k shifts up. f(x) − k shifts down.
f(x − h) shifts right. f(x + h) shifts left.
a·f(x): vertical stretch (|a|>1) or compression.
f(b·x): horizontal compression (|b|>1) or stretch.
−f(x): reflect across x-axis. f(−x): reflect across y-axis.
Inside parentheses: opposite of what you expect.
Domain: allowed inputs. Range: possible outputs.
Polynomials: domain all reals.
Rational: exclude zeros of denominator.
Square root: expression inside ≥ 0.
Find range by analyzing min/max and asymptotes.
arcsin(x) returns the angle whose sine is x.
Restricted domain so outputs are unique.
arccos: [0, π]. arctan: (-π/2, π/2).
Used to solve trig equations for angles.
Note: calculator usually gives one value — others may exist.
Double angle formulas express sin(2θ), cos(2θ) via θ.
sin(2θ) = 2 sin θ cos θ.
cos(2θ) has three equivalent forms.
Useful for integration and simplification.
Derive from sum formula with A = B.
A square matrix A has an inverse A⁻¹ if A·A⁻¹ = I.
Inverse exists iff det(A) ≠ 0.
2×2 formula: swap diagonal, negate off-diagonal, divide by det.
Use to solve Ax = b: x = A⁻¹b.
Compute for larger matrices via row reduction.
Write system as matrix equation Ax = b.
Form augmented matrix [A | b].
Row operations: swap, scale, add multiple of another.
Reduce to row echelon form. Back-substitute.
Determinant: zero means no unique solution.
A matrix can be viewed as a function that transforms vectors.
Linear means T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v).
Standard transforms: rotation, reflection, scaling, shear.
Compose transformations by multiplying matrices.
Foundation of computer graphics and quantum mechanics.
Span: all linear combinations of a set of vectors.
Linear independence: no vector is a combination of others.
Basis: an independent set that spans the space.
Any vector in the space is a unique combination of basis vectors.
Dimension: number of basis vectors.
Linear regression finds the line minimizing sum of squared errors.
Model: y = mx + b + noise.
Minimize Σ(y_i − (mx_i + b))².
Take derivative w.r.t. m and b, set to zero.
Closed form exists: m = cov(x,y)/var(x).
Underpins most of ML. Ridge/Lasso are regularized versions.
Chi-square tests whether observed counts match expected.
χ² = Σ (O - E)² / E.
Larger χ² means bigger discrepancy.
Compare against critical value or get p-value.
Used in genetics, A/B tests, survey analysis.
The Central Limit Theorem: sum of many independent random variables is approximately normal.
Does not matter what the original distribution was.
Sample mean is normally distributed with same mean and σ/√n.
Needs large enough n (usually n ≥ 30).
Why the normal distribution is so common in nature.
Foundation of most statistical inference.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Markov Chains
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Next state depends only on current.

scenes.narration: A Markov chain: future state depends only on present, not past.
Memoryless property.
Transition matrix: P[i,j] = prob of going from state i to j.
Steady-state distribution: long-run probabilities.
Used in PageRank, weather, genetics, queues.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Monte Carlo Methods
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Random sampling to solve hard problems.

scenes.narration: Monte Carlo: solve problems by random sampling.
Example: estimate π by throwing darts at a square with inscribed circle.
Integration: sample points, average function values, scale.
Works in high dimensions where grid methods fail.
Used in physics, finance, AI (MCMC, AlphaGo).
Big O is upper bound. Big Omega is lower bound.
Big Theta is tight: both upper and lower.
Mergesort: O(n log n), Ω(n log n), Θ(n log n).
Quicksort: average Θ(n log n), worst Θ(n²).
Big O is common but imprecise. Theta is exact.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Partial Derivatives
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Derivative with other variables held constant.

scenes.narration: For a multivariable function, partial derivative varies one variable, holds others constant.
f(x,y) = x²y. Partial w.r.t. x: 2xy. W.r.t. y: x².
Gradient: vector of all partial derivatives.
Points in direction of steepest increase.
Foundation of multivariable calculus and ML gradient descent.
A double integral computes volume under a surface over a 2D region.
Evaluate as iterated integral: integrate one variable at a time.
Order of integration can often be swapped (Fubini).
Change to polar for disk-shaped regions.
Used for surface area, mass, probability over 2D.
Enzymes are proteins that speed up specific reactions.
They lower activation energy — the hump to start a reaction.
Lock-and-key model: substrate fits the active site.
Activity affected by temperature, pH, and inhibitors.
Almost all cell reactions depend on enzymes.
Photosynthesis: light reactions + Calvin cycle.
Light reactions (thylakoid): water splits, O₂ released, ATP and NADPH made.
Calvin cycle (stroma): CO₂ fixed into glucose using ATP and NADPH.
Overall: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂.
Chlorophyll absorbs light (red/blue); reflects green.
Foundation of almost all life on Earth.
A virus is genetic material (DNA or RNA) wrapped in protein.
Cannot replicate alone — must hijack host cells.
Lytic cycle: destroy host. Lysogenic: integrate, dormant.
Not technically alive — no cellular machinery of their own.
Flu, HIV, COVID, HPV all viral. Antibiotics don't work on them.
Antibiotics target bacterial-specific structures (cell wall, ribosomes, DNA machinery).
They don't work on viruses.
Overuse breeds resistance: bacteria evolve immunity.
Finish your full course even when you feel better.
Antibiotic resistance is a leading global health threat.
An ecosystem includes all living organisms and their physical environment.
Biotic: organisms. Abiotic: sunlight, water, soil, climate.
Biomes are large-scale ecosystems: tundra, forest, desert, grassland.
Climate determines biome distribution.
Lose keystone species, the whole system shifts.
Symbiosis: long-term interaction between different species.
Mutualism: both benefit. Bees and flowers.
Commensalism: one benefits, the other unaffected. Barnacles on whales.
Parasitism: one benefits, other harmed. Tapeworms.
Found in every ecosystem. Drives evolution.
The nervous system processes information and coordinates responses.
Two parts: central (brain + spinal cord) and peripheral.
Neurons: signaling cells. Electrical + chemical.
Synapses: gaps where neurotransmitters cross.
Autonomic system: involuntary (heartbeat, digestion).
Endocrine system uses hormones — chemical messengers in blood.
Slower than nervous system, but longer-lasting.
Glands: pituitary, thyroid, adrenals, pancreas, gonads.
Negative feedback loops regulate levels.
Controls growth, metabolism, stress, reproduction.
Circulatory system delivers oxygen, nutrients, hormones. Removes waste.
Heart pumps blood. Four chambers in humans.
Arteries: away from heart. Veins: toward. Capillaries: exchange.
Two circuits: pulmonary (lungs) + systemic (body).
Blood: red cells (O₂), white (immune), platelets, plasma.
Respiratory system: brings oxygen in, removes carbon dioxide.
Air path: nose/mouth → trachea → bronchi → alveoli.
Alveoli: tiny sacs where O₂ enters blood, CO₂ leaves.
Diaphragm contracts → chest expands → air in.
~6 liters air, ~300 million alveoli per person.
Digestive system breaks food into nutrients the body absorbs.
Mouth → esophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine.
Stomach acid kills bacteria, breaks proteins.
Small intestine: most nutrient absorption.
Liver makes bile. Pancreas: enzymes and insulin.
Large intestine absorbs water. Eliminates waste.
Kidneys filter blood, removing waste and extra water.
Functional unit: the nephron.
Filter blood, reabsorb useful substances, excrete urine.
Also regulate blood pressure and electrolyte balance.
Two kidneys process ~50 gallons blood per day.
Three types of muscle in your body.
Skeletal: voluntary, striped. Attached to bones.
Cardiac: in the heart. Striped, involuntary, rhythmic.
Smooth: in organs. Unstriped, involuntary.
Muscles contract by actin-myosin interaction.
Energy from ATP. Fatigue follows long effort.
The skeleton supports the body, protects organs, enables movement.
206 bones in adult humans.
Bones produce blood cells (red marrow) and store calcium.
Joints: places where bones meet. Hinge, ball-and-socket.
Bones are living tissue — constantly remodeled.
Light enters the eye through cornea, lens focuses on retina.
Retina has photoreceptors: rods (dim light) and cones (color).
Three cone types: red, green, blue. Mixed to perceive all colors.
Signals travel optic nerve to visual cortex.
Brain constructs the image you "see".
Sound waves enter the ear canal, vibrate the eardrum.
Three tiny bones amplify vibration to the cochlea.
Cochlea: fluid-filled spiral with hair cells.
Different frequencies activate different hair cells.
Nerves carry signals to auditory cortex.
Homeostasis: maintaining stable internal conditions despite external change.
Temperature, pH, blood sugar, water balance — all regulated.
Negative feedback: sensor + effector correct deviation.
Sweating cools. Shivering warms. Thirst drives drinking.
Break homeostasis → disease.
Four laws govern thermodynamics.
Zeroth: if A = B and B = C in temperature, A = C.
First: energy conserved. ΔU = Q − W.
Second: entropy of universe increases.
Third: at absolute zero, entropy approaches minimum.
Rules why machines can't be 100% efficient, why heat flows.
Heat moves three ways.
Conduction: direct contact. Metal spoon in hot soup.
Convection: fluid movement. Hot air rises.
Radiation: electromagnetic waves. Sun warms you.
Home heating uses all three.
Fluid dynamics studies how liquids and gases move.
Continuity: mass flow rate is constant. A₁v₁ = A₂v₂.
Bernoulli: faster flow → lower pressure.
Airplane lift: air faster over top of wing → lower pressure.
Also explains curving balls, pipe narrowing.
Archimedes: buoyant force equals weight of displaced fluid.
Float if body density < fluid density.
Ice floats on water because it's less dense.
Submarines adjust tanks of water/air to dive or surface.
True for gases too. Hot air balloons rise in cooler air.
Fission splits a heavy nucleus into lighter ones.
Uranium-235 absorbs a neutron, splits, releases energy + more neutrons.
Chain reaction can sustain if enough material (critical mass).
Reactor: controlled chain reaction. Bomb: uncontrolled.
Energy per reaction: millions of times chemical combustion.
Fusion combines light nuclei (hydrogen) into heavier (helium).
Releases more energy per mass than fission.
Requires extreme heat and pressure (stars) to overcome repulsion.
Humans: ITER reactor attempting sustained fusion.
Clean, abundant — if we ever fully crack it.
Special relativity: laws of physics same for all inertial observers.
Speed of light is constant — same for every observer.
Time dilates for moving observers.
Length contracts in direction of motion.
Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc².
Experimentally confirmed in atomic clocks, particle accelerators.
General relativity: gravity isn't a force — it's curved spacetime.
Massive objects curve the fabric of spacetime around them.
Objects follow straight paths in curved space (geodesics).
Predictions: gravitational lensing, time dilation near mass, black holes.
Tested in Mercury's orbit, GPS satellites, LIGO.
Quantum mechanics: very small particles don't follow classical rules.
Particles like electrons behave as both waves and particles.
Described by wavefunctions. |ψ|² gives probability.
Uncertainty principle: can't know position and momentum exactly.
Observation changes the system.
Underlies chemistry, electronics, lasers, MRI.
A black hole is a region where gravity is so strong nothing escapes.
Event horizon: boundary of no return.
Formed when massive stars collapse.
Sagittarius A*: supermassive black hole at Milky Way center.
First image captured 2019 (M87), 2022 (Sgr A*).
Light bends around them. Time slows near them.
The Big Bang: the universe started ~13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense state.
Rapid expansion (inflation) followed.
Evidence: cosmic microwave background, redshift of galaxies.
Formed light elements (H, He) in first minutes. Heavier came later from stars.
Universe still expanding, accelerating due to dark energy.
Our best model for cosmic origin. Confirmed by many independent tests.
Visible matter makes up only ~5% of the universe.
~27% is dark matter. Invisible, but gravitates.
Observed via gravitational effects on galaxies.
~68% is dark energy. Drives accelerating expansion.
We don't know what either actually is.
A string is a sequence of characters.
Common methods: length, indexOf, substring, split, replace.
Immutable in most languages: you get new strings, not modifications.
Concatenation joins: "a" + "b" = "ab".
Regex offers powerful pattern matching.
Objects (or dictionaries/hash maps) store key-value pairs.
Access by key, not index. Fast lookup.
Keys: strings in JS, anything hashable in Python.
Values: any type. Even nested objects.
JSON is essentially a string representation of objects.
A class is a template for creating objects with shared structure and behavior.
Constructor initializes each instance.
Methods: functions on instances.
Properties: data attached to instance.
Subclasses inherit and can override.
Inheritance: class B inherits properties and methods of A.
B can override or extend A's behavior.
Polymorphism: objects of different classes respond to same interface.
Example: animal.speak() — dogs bark, cats meow.
Enables writing flexible, reusable code.
Abstract class: can't be instantiated directly. Defines common interface + some implementation.
Interface: purely a contract. No implementation.
Classes implement interfaces by providing all required methods.
Multiple interface implementation allowed. Multiple inheritance often isn't.
Used for dependency injection, mocking, testing.
Exceptions are errors that disrupt normal flow.
try: code that might fail. catch: handle it.
finally: runs regardless. Cleanup.
Throw custom exceptions for expected error cases.
Don't swallow errors silently. Always log or handle.
Regex: a pattern language for matching text.
[abc]: any of a, b, c. \d: digit. \w: word char. \s: whitespace.
*: zero or more. +: one or more. ?: zero or one.
^: start. $: end. (): group.
Email regex: ~40 chars can validate most emails.
Indispensable for text processing.
File I/O: programs read from and write to storage.
Open → read/write → close.
Text mode vs binary mode.
Use buffered I/O for performance.
Handle errors: file not found, permissions.
Modern APIs use async for responsiveness.
TCP/IP is the protocol suite the internet runs on.
IP: routing packets. Each device has an IP address.
TCP: reliable, ordered delivery.
UDP: faster, unreliable, for video/games.
Packets are chunks of data — reassembled at destination.
Every web request rides on top of TCP/IP.
DNS: Domain Name System. Turns names into IPs.
Hierarchical: root → TLD → domain.
Cached at multiple levels for speed.
Distributed — no single point of failure.
Without DNS, you'd type IP addresses.
Caching: store results to avoid recomputing or refetching.
CPU cache, memory, disk, network — all layers cache.
Cache hit: found in cache. Cache miss: had to compute/fetch.
Invalidation: when cached data becomes stale.
Phil Karlton: "Only two hard things — naming and cache invalidation."
Containers package apps with their dependencies.
Runs consistently across dev, test, prod.
Lightweight vs VMs: share host OS kernel.
Docker: most popular container tech.
Kubernetes orchestrates many containers at scale.
Microservices: split an application into small, independently deployable services.
Each service owns its data and has a focused responsibility.
Services communicate via APIs.
Benefits: scale independently, deploy independently.
Costs: complexity, distributed debugging, ops overhead.
Right when teams grow beyond one codebase's capacity.
CSS: Cascading Style Sheets. Controls how HTML is displayed.
Rules: selector { property: value; }.
Box model: content → padding → border → margin.
Flexbox and Grid for layout.
Media queries for responsive design.
Modern web tools like Tailwind build on CSS.
JavaScript is single-threaded but handles async via the event loop.
Call stack: what's running now.
Callbacks wait in the task queue.
When stack empty, event loop dequeues a callback.
Promises go to the microtask queue (higher priority).
Lets JS stay responsive while doing I/O.
A Promise represents a future value.
States: pending, fulfilled, rejected.
.then() handles fulfillment. .catch() handles errors.
async/await: cleaner syntax for Promise chains.
Makes async code read like synchronous.
Typical workflow: branch, commit, push, pull request.
Create a branch for each feature.
Commit often with clear messages.
Push to remote. Open pull request.
Review, merge, delete branch.
Main branch stays deployable.
Agile: incremental, flexible development.
Scrum: common Agile framework.
Sprints: 1-4 week work cycles.
Daily standups: quick team sync.
Sprint planning, review, retrospective each cycle.
Adapts to changing requirements better than waterfall.
Unit tests: test individual functions in isolation.
Integration tests: test components working together.
End-to-end tests: simulate user flow through the app.
Test pyramid: many unit, fewer integration, few e2e.
TDD: write test first, then code to pass it.
Good tests prevent regressions.
JOINs combine rows from multiple tables.
INNER JOIN: only matching rows in both tables.
LEFT JOIN: all rows from left, matched right (nulls if no match).
RIGHT JOIN: mirror of LEFT.
FULL JOIN: all rows from both, matched where possible.
Almost all real queries use JOINs.
NoSQL: any database not based on relational model.
Document stores: MongoDB. JSON-like.
Key-value: Redis, DynamoDB. Ultra-fast lookups.
Graph: Neo4j. Relationships are first-class.
Column-store: Cassandra. Distributed analytics.
Trade strict schema for flexibility and scale.
ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
Atomicity: all-or-nothing.
Consistency: valid state → valid state.
Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere.
Durability: once committed, persists.
Traditional RDBMS guarantee all four.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: CAP Theorem
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Pick two: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.

scenes.narration: CAP theorem: distributed systems trade off between these three.
Consistency: every read gets latest write.
Availability: every request gets response.
Partition tolerance: system works despite network splits.
In a partition, you must sacrifice either C or A.
Design choice depends on use case.
Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data.
Clustering: group similar items. K-means, hierarchical.
Dimensionality reduction: PCA, t-SNE, UMAP.
Anomaly detection: find outliers.
Applications: customer segmentation, fraud detection, recommendation.
Decision trees split data by features at each node.
Each split maximizes information gain.
Easy to interpret: visible rules.
Prone to overfitting — prune or limit depth.
Random Forests: ensemble of many trees.
Great default for tabular data.
CNNs process images by sliding filters over them.
Convolution: detects local patterns (edges, textures).
Pooling: reduces size, adds translation invariance.
Stacked: each layer builds on previous (edges → parts → objects).
ResNet, VGG, Inception: famous architectures.
Foundation of computer vision.
Transformers process sequences using attention mechanisms.
Attention: each token looks at all others, weighted by relevance.
No recurrence. Parallelizable. Fast to train.
BERT, GPT, Gemini all built on transformers.
Dominant architecture for NLP, now vision and multimodal too.
Prompt engineering: crafting effective inputs to LLMs.
Be specific. Give context. State the task clearly.
Few-shot: provide examples of desired outputs.
Chain-of-thought: ask for step-by-step reasoning.
Role-play: "You are an expert..."
Iterate. Prompt engineering is experimental.
Information security rests on three pillars: the CIA triad.
Confidentiality: only authorized access.
Integrity: data not modified.
Availability: accessible when needed.
Attacks target one or more. Defenses protect all three.
Add AAA: Authentication, Authorization, Audit.
XSS: Cross-Site Scripting. Attacker injects JavaScript into pages.
Defense: escape output, use CSP headers.
CSRF: Cross-Site Request Forgery. Trick user into making unintended request.
Defense: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies.
Always validate and sanitize user input.
SQL injection: attacker inserts SQL into input.
Example: enter " OR 1=1 --" in password field.
If not escaped, the query becomes always-true.
Defense: parameterized queries, ORMs.
Never concatenate user input into SQL.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Writing a Summary
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Distill a source into its essence.

scenes.narration: A summary captures main ideas in much less space than the original.
Read first. Identify thesis and key supporting points.
State main ideas in your own words.
Omit examples and minor details.
Don't add your opinion — that's critique, not summary.
A good summary ~1/5 to 1/10 of the original.
An outline organizes your arguments before prose.
Start with thesis.
List main supporting arguments.
Under each, list evidence/examples.
Plan counterarguments and rebuttals.
Rearrange for best flow before drafting.
The first sentence should hook the reader.
Types: surprising fact, vivid anecdote, provocative question, bold claim.
Avoid: "In this essay, I will..." and dictionary-definition openings.
Set up context quickly.
End intro with thesis.
You have 10 seconds to interest a reader.
A good conclusion synthesizes — doesn't just summarize.
Restate thesis in new words.
Link back to hook if possible.
Expand to broader implications.
End memorably — image, question, call to action.
Avoid: "In conclusion..." or introducing new ideas.
Tone: attitude toward the subject. Register: level of formality.
Academic: formal, objective, precise.
Professional email: friendly but formal.
Creative: freer, can be playful or dark.
Match tone to audience and purpose.
Read aloud to catch tone mismatches.
Concise writing: maximum meaning, minimum words.
Cut: "there is/are" constructions.
Cut: redundant adjectives ("very unique").
Cut: wordy phrases. "Due to the fact that" → "because".
Prefer strong verbs over weak + adverb.
Every word should earn its place.
Show, don't tell: use concrete sensory detail.
Tell: "She was nervous."
Show: "Her hands trembled as she held the letter."
Engages reader's imagination. More memorable.
Overused in bad fiction — still the best rule.
Good dialogue reveals character and moves the story.
Each character should sound distinct.
Use "said" mostly. Avoid colorful speaker tags ("he exclaimed").
Break long speeches with action or beat.
Real speech is edited for readability. Remove filler.
Subtext > text. What's NOT said matters.
Setting is where and when your story happens.
Use sensory details: sight, sound, smell, taste, touch.
Setting can reflect mood or character.
Don't info-dump. Weave detail into action.
In fantasy/sci-fi, rules of the world matter. Be consistent.
POV: the narrative perspective.
First person: "I went to the store." Intimate.
Second person: "You walk into the room." Rare.
Third limited: one character's thoughts.
Third omniscient: narrator knows all.
Stick with one POV within a scene. Shifts confuse.
Connect abstract ideas to concrete examples to understand them.
Exponential growth? Compound interest. Covid spread.
Examples are easier to remember than definitions.
Use multiple examples to generalize correctly.
After understanding, try to create your own examples.
Study groups amplify learning — when run well.
Limit to 3-5 people, each prepared in advance.
Take turns teaching topics — Feynman-style.
Work problems together, compare approaches.
Avoid: groups that devolve into socializing.
Set a time limit. Meet consistently.
Don't read textbooks like novels.
Survey first: table of contents, chapter summaries.
Read objectives / questions before the chapter.
Read actively: note key points, highlight sparingly.
Do problems. Concepts without practice fade fast.
Review: summarize chapter in your words.

visual.text: SQ3R: survey, question, read, recite, review.

scenes.narration: Notes should capture ideas, not transcribe verbatim.
Abbreviate. Use symbols: → = "leads to", ∴ = "therefore".
Leave margins for questions and later additions.
Review within 24 hours to solidify memory.
Record lectures if allowed — rewatch hard parts.
Typing captures more; handwriting encodes better.
An error log tracks the mistakes you make — and why.
For each wrong answer, write: problem, mistake, correct approach.
Patterns emerge: same type of error repeats.
Review before tests. Confront weak spots directly.
The fastest way to stop making the same mistake.
Metacognition: awareness of your own thinking and learning.
Ask yourself: do I actually understand, or am I familiar?
Recognize when you're confused. Don't skip past.
Plan: what strategy fits this problem?
Monitor: is this working? Course-correct.
Top students do this unconsciously. It can be trained.
Curious people learn faster and remember longer.
Ask "why?" often, even for things you already know.
Follow tangents that interest you.
Read broadly. Connect across fields.
Studies show dopamine from curiosity aids memory.
Find what you love learning. You'll do more of it.
Reward yourself for hitting study milestones.
Immediate small rewards build habits.
Examples: coffee break, YouTube video, 10 min game.
Bigger rewards for bigger milestones: dinner out for A on exam.
Avoid: rewards that take over (3-hour gaming "break").
Good sleep dramatically improves learning.
Consistent schedule: same bed/wake times.
Dark, cool room.
No screens 30-60 min before bed (blue light).
No caffeine after noon. No alcohol before sleep.
Aim 7-9 hours. Cramming kills it.
Brain uses ~20% of body's energy. Food fuels it.
Prefer complex carbs for steady energy.
Protein stabilizes energy. Fats (omega-3) support brain.
Hydrate. Dehydration causes fatigue and fog.
Avoid sugar crashes. Limit big caffeine doses.
Sugary snacks feel productive. Aren't.
Repeated factors: higher-power denominators.
For (x − a)²: include A/(x − a) + B/(x − a)².
For irreducible quadratic (x² + bx + c): use Ax + B / (x² + bx + c).
More terms, but same algebra: equate and solve.
Complete toolkit for any rational integral.
Newton's method uses derivatives to find roots iteratively.
Update: x_{n+1} = x_n − f(x_n)/f'(x_n).
Converges quadratically: each step roughly doubles precision.
Can fail: derivative = 0, poor start, multiple roots.
Calculators and optimization use this heavily.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Combinatorial Proofs
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Counting the same thing two ways.

scenes.narration: A combinatorial proof shows two expressions count the same thing.
Example: C(n, k) = C(n, n−k). Count subsets of size k = count of their complements.
Pascal's identity: C(n, k) = C(n-1, k-1) + C(n-1, k). Partition by whether last element is included.
Elegant and often easier than algebraic proof.
Chaos theory: simple deterministic systems can produce unpredictable behavior.
Tiny changes in initial conditions → wildly different outcomes.
Lorenz system: models weather. Three equations, chaotic output.
Strange attractors: systems bounded but never repeating exactly.
Weather, populations, turbulence all chaotic.
Epigenetics: heritable changes in gene expression that don't alter DNA sequence.
DNA methylation silences genes.
Histone modifications change chromatin accessibility.
Environment can leave epigenetic marks.
Some marks pass to offspring.
COVID is caused by SARS-CoV-2, a coronavirus.
Spike protein binds to ACE2 receptors on human cells.
Virus enters, hijacks cell to replicate.
Spreads mostly through respiratory droplets.
mRNA vaccines instruct cells to make spike protein, training immunity.
Masks, ventilation, vaccines all reduce transmission.
Cancer: cells grow and divide uncontrollably.
Starts with mutations in genes controlling growth or DNA repair.
Oncogenes drive growth. Tumor suppressors normally restrain.
Multiple mutations accumulate over time.
Metastasis: cancer spreads to distant sites.
Screening, prevention, and new immunotherapies help.
CRISPR is a tool to edit DNA with precision.
Guide RNA directs Cas9 enzyme to specific DNA location.
Cas9 cuts. Cell repairs with errors or with supplied template.
Revolutionized genetics. Fast, cheap, precise.
Treats genetic diseases. Ethical questions on human embryos.
Nobel Prize 2020.
Quantum computing uses qubits, which can be in superposition.
A qubit represents 0 and 1 simultaneously.
Entanglement: qubits correlate across distance.
Certain problems (factoring, simulation) dramatically faster.
Shor's algorithm breaks RSA.
Current machines: noisy, small. Large useful ones: 10+ years off.
Compilers translate source code to machine code ahead of time.
Interpreters execute source code directly, line by line.
Compiled: C, Rust, Go — fast, early type checks.
Interpreted: Python, Ruby, JS — flexible, slower.
JIT (just-in-time) compilation: best of both worlds.
V8 (JS) and JVM (Java) are JIT-based.
Programs use stack and heap memory.
Stack: fast, LIFO, function call data.
Heap: dynamic allocation, slower, manual or GC.
Manual (C): malloc/free. Easy to leak.
Garbage collection (Java, JS): automatic cleanup.
Rust: ownership system ensures safety without GC.
Design patterns are proven solutions to recurring design problems.
Singleton: one instance only.
Factory: creates objects without specifying exact class.
Observer: publish-subscribe notifications.
Strategy: swap algorithms at runtime.
From Gang of Four book. Don't overuse — simple often best.
Functional programming treats functions as primary building blocks.
Pure functions: same input → same output. No side effects.
Immutability: data doesn't change. New data created.
Higher-order functions: functions as arguments or returns.
Languages: Haskell, Elixir. Features: React, many modern JS.
Easier to reason about. Popular in concurrency.
Accessibility (a11y): design so all users, including disabled, can use.
Semantic HTML: use the right tags.
Alt text for images. Screen readers need them.
Keyboard navigation: all functions without mouse.
Color contrast meets WCAG standards.
Benefits everyone — clearer content for all users.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Open Source Software
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: CS
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Code you can inspect and modify.

scenes.narration: Open source: code is publicly available and modifiable.
Licenses: MIT, Apache (permissive), GPL (copyleft).
Linux, Python, Git, React all open source.
Benefits: community, transparency, no vendor lock-in.
Contributing teaches you tons.
Powers most of the internet.
Compare-contrast essays examine similarities and differences.
Two structures: point-by-point or block.
Point-by-point: alternate between subjects by criterion.
Block: all of one subject, then all of the other.
Point-by-point works for detailed analysis; block for overview.
Use transitions: similarly, in contrast, however.
Analytical essays break down a subject to understand it.
Clear thesis that makes an analytical claim.
Break into components. Examine each.
Support with evidence from the text/source.
Analyze, don't just summarize. Explain what evidence means.
Synthesize in conclusion.
Scientific research papers use IMRaD format.
Introduction: context, gap, research question.
Methods: how you did the research.
Results: what you found (no interpretation).
Discussion: what results mean; limitations; future work.
Precise, formal, evidence-based.
Speeches are written for the ear, not the eye.
Shorter sentences. Rhythm matters.
Repetition for emphasis ("I have a dream...").
Stories land harder than statistics.
Strong opening. Strong close. Audience remembers edges.
Rehearse aloud. Time yourself.
Journalism: answer who, what, when, where, why, how.
Inverted pyramid: most important info first.
Lead (first paragraph) summarizes the story.
Further details in decreasing importance.
Objective tone. Sources cited. Quotes verified.
Reader can stop anywhere and still have the story.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Cover Letters
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Writing
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Making your case for a job.

scenes.narration: A cover letter explains why you're right for a specific role.
Open with hook — not "I am writing to apply".
Show genuine knowledge of the company.
Match your skills to the job's needs with specific examples.
End with clear next step: I'd love to interview.
One page. Tailored per application.
Pareto principle: 80% of results come from 20% of effort.
In studying: some topics yield disproportionate results.
Identify high-yield content: past exams, syllabus priorities.
Master fundamentals before advanced details.
Not always 80/20 exactly — but often close.
Two-minute rule: if it takes less than 2 min, do it now.
Second version: start a habit with just 2 min.
Want to read more? Read 2 min a day.
Starting is the hardest part. Lower the barrier.
Often you'll keep going. Sometimes 2 min is enough.
Strategic test-taking can add significant points.
Read ALL instructions before starting.
Skim the whole test. Plan time.
Do easy questions first. Build confidence.
Skip hard questions, come back. Don't get stuck.
Leave 5-10 min to review at the end.
For MCQ: guess if no penalty, eliminate wrong options first.
Bloom's taxonomy: hierarchy of learning depth.
Remember: recall facts.
Understand: explain in own words.
Apply: use in new situations.
Analyze: break apart, identify relationships.
Evaluate: judge, defend.
Create: produce new, original work.
Study at the level you'll be tested.
Chunking: group related info into meaningful units.
Phone numbers are chunked: 555-123-4567, not 5551234567.
Working memory holds ~7 chunks (not items).
Chunking expands effective memory by chunking smarter.
Experts chunk automatically in their domain.
Learn to see higher-level patterns to chunk better.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Energy Management
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Study Skills
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Time is less important than energy.

scenes.narration: Time management is just half the equation. Energy matters more.
Everyone has daily cycles of alertness.
Do your hardest work during your peak hours.
Save routine tasks (email, filing) for low-energy times.
Don't try to push through exhaustion — effectiveness plummets.
Sleep, food, exercise — all feed your energy.
External: notifications, noise, people.
Internal: wandering thoughts, boredom, anxiety.
External: eliminate. Mute, move, hide.
Internal: harder. Notice → redirect.
Meditation trains the "notice → redirect" muscle.
Focus is a skill. Like any muscle, it strengthens with practice.
Deep work: focused, undistracted work on cognitively demanding tasks.
Rare in modern life. Notifications, meetings, fragmentation.
But also increasingly valuable. Pays outsized rewards.
Block 2-4 hour sessions. No interruptions.
Work in a dedicated space. Phone away.
Cal Newport's book popularized the term.
Reflective journaling: regular writing about what you've learned and how.
Forces you to articulate tacit understanding.
Surfaces gaps and confusion you didn't know about.
Tracks progress over time.
Even 5-10 minutes a day has effect.
Writers like Darwin, Einstein, da Vinci kept notebooks.
When y is tangled with x, solve for dy/dx implicitly.
Differentiate both sides with respect to x; use chain rule on y.
Example: x² + y² = 25. Differentiate: 2x + 2y·dy/dx = 0.
Solve for dy/dx: dy/dx = −x/y.
Useful for implicitly-defined curves like circles, ellipses.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Improper Integrals
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Integrals to infinity or over discontinuities.

scenes.narration: Improper integrals have infinite limits or integrands.
Infinite limit: replace ∞ with b, take limit.
Discontinuity: integrate up to it, take limit.
If the limit exists (finite), integral converges.
Useful in probability (total = 1 over all space).
L'Hôpital's rule evaluates indeterminate limits.
Form 0/0 or ∞/∞: take derivatives of top and bottom.
Example: lim (sin x)/x as x→0. Derivatives: cos x / 1 → 1.
Can apply multiple times if still indeterminate.
Must verify indeterminate form first.
Rotate a curve around an axis. The resulting solid's volume?
Slice into thin disks. Each: π r² dx.
Example: rotate y = x² from 0 to 1 around x-axis. V = π/5.
Washer method: if there's a hole, subtract inner radius².
Shell method: alternative using cylindrical shells.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Parametric Equations
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Math
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: x and y both depend on t.

scenes.narration: Parametric equations: both x and y are functions of a parameter t.
Think of t as time — trace the curve as t increases.
Example: x = cos t, y = sin t traces a circle.
Derivative: dy/dx = (dy/dt) / (dx/dt).
Useful for paths that aren't functions (loops, self-intersections).
A vector field assigns a vector to every point in space.
Wind velocity, fluid flow, gravity — all vector fields.
Gradient of a scalar field is a vector field.
Divergence: how much is flowing out. Curl: rotation.
Stokes' theorem and divergence theorem connect them.
Stokes' theorem: line integral around a boundary equals flux of curl through surface.
Generalization of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Central to electromagnetism (Maxwell's equations).
Divergence theorem: similar for flux + volumes.
Beautiful unifier of vector calculus.
Genetic drift: random change in allele frequencies.
Stronger in small populations.
Not adaptive — just chance.
Bottleneck effect: population shrinks drastically. Remaining alleles over-represented.
Founder effect: small migrant population shapes new gene pool.
Alongside selection and mutation, drives evolution.
After exposure to a pathogen, memory cells persist.
Next exposure: fast, strong response. May not get sick.
Vaccines expose immune system to harmless versions.
Types: live attenuated, inactivated, subunit, mRNA.
mRNA vaccines teach your cells to make viral protein briefly.
Saved more lives than almost any other intervention.
Stem cells can differentiate into various cell types.
Totipotent: any cell (early embryo).
Pluripotent: many cell types (blastocyst).
Multipotent: limited (adult bone marrow).
iPSCs: induced pluripotent — reprogrammed adult cells. Nobel 2012.
Promise for regenerative medicine.
Four mechanisms drive evolutionary change.
Natural selection: differential survival.
Genetic drift: random allele change.
Mutation: new alleles appear.
Gene flow: alleles move between populations.
All four interact. Selection is the directional one.
Kinetics studies how fast reactions proceed.
Rate law: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n.
Order is sum of exponents.
Factors: concentration, temperature, catalysts, surface area.
Activation energy: barrier to reaction start.
Arrhenius equation connects temperature and rate.

VIDEO_LESSONS.title: Colligative Properties
VIDEO_LESSONS.subject: Science
VIDEO_LESSONS.description: Solution properties depend on particle count.

scenes.narration: Colligative properties depend on number of solute particles, not type.
Boiling point elevation: adding solute raises BP.
Freezing point depression: adding solute lowers FP.
Vapor pressure lowered.
Osmotic pressure develops across semipermeable membrane.
Salt on icy roads. Antifreeze. Osmosis in cells.
Redox reactions involve electron transfer.
Split into two half-reactions: oxidation and reduction.
Balance atoms, then charge, then electrons.
Multiply half-reactions so electrons cancel.
Add them back together.
Works in acidic and basic solutions.
Nuclear reactions change the nucleus itself.
Alpha decay: nucleus emits helium-4.
Beta decay: neutron → proton + electron.
Gamma: high-energy photon from excited nucleus.
Mass converts to energy: E = Δm c².
Powers stars, reactors, medical imaging.
Newton's law of gravitation: every mass attracts every other.
G is the gravitational constant (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹).
Falls with square of distance.
Explains falling objects, planetary orbits, tides.
Refined by Einstein, but Newton works for most purposes.
Pressure in a fluid increases with depth.
ρ is density. g is gravity. h is depth.
Atmospheric pressure at sea level: ~101 kPa.
Same at the same depth horizontally.
Explains why ears pop when diving or flying.
Earth is ~4.6 billion years old.
Scale: eons → eras → periods → epochs.
Major events: formation of Earth, oxygen in atmosphere, Cambrian explosion, mass extinctions, humans.
We live in the Holocene. Some argue Anthropocene.
Humans: last ~2 seconds of the cosmic day.
Electric power: rate of energy flow.
Unit: watt = joule per second.
Energy = power × time.
kWh (kilowatt-hour): common unit on electric bills.
LED bulbs use 10 W. Fridge ~150 W. Electric car charging ~10,000 W.
AI is accelerating science across fields.
AlphaFold predicted ~every protein structure.
AI helps find new drugs, materials, particle physics.
Climate models run with ML acceleration.
Risks: hallucination, misuse, bias. Care needed.
A new scientific instrument, like the microscope.
Webhooks: HTTP callbacks that fire when events happen.
You give a URL. Service calls it when something happens.
Examples: Stripe payment complete, GitHub PR opened.
More efficient than polling.
Must verify source (usually signed with secret).
GraphQL: query language for APIs.
Unlike REST, client specifies exactly what data to return.
Single endpoint. One request gets all needed data.
Schema defined by server.
Facebook, GitHub, Shopify use it.
Trade-off: complex caching, steeper learning curve.
WebSockets: persistent, bidirectional connection between browser and server.
Unlike HTTP (request/response), server can push anytime.
Used for real-time: chat, live updates, games.
Lower overhead than polling.
Upgrade from HTTP to WebSocket via handshake.
Socket.io is a popular JS library.
Service workers run in the background, independent of a web page.
Enable offline functionality via cache.
Handle push notifications.
Intercept network requests.
Core of Progressive Web Apps.
Installed on first visit. Updated in background.
React hooks let functional components use state and lifecycle.
useState: add state to a component.
useEffect: run side effects on mount/update.
useContext: access context without prop drilling.
Custom hooks: reuse logic across components.
Introduced in React 16.8. Changed how we write components.
TypeScript: superset of JavaScript that adds static types.
Types catch bugs at compile time, not runtime.
Better autocomplete, refactoring, navigation.
Types erased at compile time. Final output is JS.
Gradually adoptable. Add types to JS incrementally.
Standard for large JS codebases.
Paradigms: ways to think about and structure code.
Imperative: do this, then this. C, assembly.
Object-oriented: objects with state and methods. Java.
Functional: pure functions. Haskell, Elixir.
Declarative: describe what you want. SQL, React JSX.
Most modern languages blend paradigms.
Rust guarantees memory safety without garbage collection.
Ownership: each value has one owner at a time.
Borrowing: temporary references, checked at compile time.
Compiler enforces rules — no null, no data races.
Speed of C. Safety of garbage-collected languages.
Powering systems: Firefox, Dropbox, Windows kernel parts.
AI systems make decisions affecting people. Ethics matters.
Bias: models inherit biases from training data.
Fairness: different groups should be treated equitably.
Transparency: why did the model decide this?
Privacy: what data is collected and how used.
Alignment: AI goals match human goals.
Engineers, not just ethicists, have responsibility.
Editing has phases. Don't do them all at once.
Big-picture first: structure, argument, flow.
Then paragraph-level: topic sentences, transitions.
Sentence-level: clarity, concision, rhythm.
Final: proofread for typos, grammar.
Read aloud to catch awkward passages.
Plagiarism: using someone else's ideas/words without credit.
Direct copying without quotes: always plagiarism.
Paraphrasing without citing: also plagiarism.
Common knowledge doesn't need citation.
When in doubt, cite.
AI-generated content: check your institution's policy.
Your environment strongly influences study behavior.
Have a dedicated study space, ideally with good lighting.
Keep it tidy — cluttered space distracts.
Remove distractions: phone elsewhere, tabs closed.
Same place triggers associative state: "here I focus."
Library, café — move if you need variety.
Passive learning: watch, read, listen.
Active learning: answer, solve, teach, create.
Active retrieval typically produces two to three times better retention than passive re-reading (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006).
Convert passive to active: close book, recall; explain out loud; make flashcards.
Watch lecture once. Then study actively.
Passive feels easier. Active works.
